revision.text:
Taga Jinja or Taga Taisha
English Name: Taga Shrine Address: Taga-cho Mailing Address: City: Inukami-gun State: Shiga-ken Postal Code: 522-03 Country: JAPAN Latitude/Longitude: lat=35.2; long=136.333 Find Gardens Nearby Phone: Fax: E-Mail: 35� 12' 00" N 136� 20' 00 Contact: Designer(s): Contruction Date: Momoyama Public/Private: PUBLIC
Location: southeast side of Lake Biwa, this ancient shrine is old enough that it was mentioned in the kojiki; built during a time when Buddhism and Shinto were both doing very well. It contains both a Buddhist temple and Shinto shrine. The garden here was constructed by Toyotomi Hideyoshi in the Tensei era (1573-1591) as an offering for the recovery of his wife from illness. It is next to the study (shoin) attached to the Shrine offices (shamusho). There is a stone bridge and a sanzon stone arrangement.
The shrine is also known for its harvest festival (hokenko shuki taisai)held around the end of November.
The site is a 20 minute walk from Taga Station, Omi Tetsudo Line (JR), or a bus from JR Minami Hikone Station, Biwako Line.
<gallery> Image:Taga taisha1.JPG| </gallery>
[[User:Trnscndr|Trnscndr]] 07:16, 8 January 2007 (UTC)Bryan E. Hall, MPW Professor of Crtical Thinking Clayton State University Morrow/Atlanta, GA

{{orphan|date=November 2007}}
'''Taga Taisha''' is a [[Shinto]] [[Jinja (Shinto)|shrine]] in [[Japan]]. It is located on the southeast side of [[Lake Biwa]].
This ancient shrine is old enough that it was mentioned in the ''[[Kojiki]]''. According to that text it dates from AD620, having been built during a time when [[Buddhism]] and Shinto were both doing very well. It contains both a Buddhist temple and Shinto shrine. The garden here was constructed by [[Toyotomi Hideyoshi]] in the Tensei era (1573-1591) as an offering for the recovery of his wife from illness. It is next to the study (''shoin'') attached to the Shrine offices (''shamusho''). There is a stone bridge and a sanzon stone arrangement.
The shrine is also known for its harvest festival (''hokenko shuki taisai'') held around the end of November.

revision.comment: moved [[Taga taisha]] to [[Taga Taisha]]: Proper name of place, also to match other "Taisha" entries
revision.text:
{{orphan|date=November 2007}}
'''Taga Taisha''' is a [[Shinto]] [[Jinja (Shinto)|shrine]] in [[Japan]]. It is located on the southeast side of [[Lake Biwa]].
This ancient shrine is old enough that it was mentioned in the ''[[Kojiki]]''. According to that text it dates from AD620, having been built during a time when [[Buddhism]] and Shinto were both doing very well. It contains both a Buddhist temple and Shinto shrine. The garden here was constructed by [[Toyotomi Hideyoshi]] in the Tensei era (1573-1591) as an offering for the recovery of his wife from illness. It is next to the study (''shoin'') attached to the Shrine offices (''shamusho''). There is a stone bridge and a sanzon stone arrangement.
The shrine is also known for its harvest festival (''hokenko shuki taisai'') held around the end of November.
<gallery> Image:Taga taisha1.JPG|Main Shrine Image:Tagataishasmallshrine1.JPG|Small Shrine with ancient artifact horse statue Image:Kaguraden-taga-taisha.JPG|Kagura-den (noh stage) for small blessing ceremonies and social activities Image:Tagataisha-rockgarden.JPG|Rock Garden Image:Tagataisharockbridge.JPG|Stone Bridge </gallery>
*English Name: Taga Shrine *Address: Taga-cho *Mailing Address: *City: [[Inukami District, Shiga|Inukami-gun]] *State/Prefecture: [[Shiga Prefecture|Shiga-ken]] *Postal Code: 522-03 *Country: JAPAN *Latitude/Longitude: lat=35.2; long=136.333 (35.2 12' 00" N 136.333 20' 00) *Construction Date: Around AD620 (According to the Kojiki) *Public/Private: PUBLIC * Access: The site is a 20 minute walk from Taga Station, Omi Tetsudo Line (JR), or a bus from JR Minami Hikone Station, Biwako Line.

{{unreferenced}} {{Infobox Shinto shrine | name = Taga-taisha
多賀大社 | image = Taga taisha1.JPG | caption = ''[[Honden]]'' of Taga-taisha | type = | dedication = [[Izanagi]], [[Izanami]] | founded = 660 | closed = | founder = | priest = | address = Taga, [[Taga, Shiga|Taga-chō]], [[Inukami District, Shiga|Inukami-gun]], [[Shiga Prefecture|Shiga-ken]]
522-0341 | phone = 0749-48-1101 | website = http://www.tagataisya.or.jp/ }} {{nihongo|'''Taga-taisha'''|多賀大社}}, also known as {{nihongo|'''Taga-jinja'''|多賀神社}}, is a [[Shinto]] [[Jinja (Shinto)|shrine]] located in [[Taga, Shiga|Taga]], [[Shiga Prefecture]], [[Japan]] dedicated to [[Izanagi]] and [[Izanami]]. It is associated with long life, successful marriage and good fortune. The shrine is frequently referred to as {{nihongo|O-taga-san|お多賀さん}} by local residents. ==History== This ancient shrine is old to have been mentioned in the ''[[Kojiki]]''. According to that text it dates from [[620]], having been built during a time when [[Buddhism]] and Shinto were both doing very well. It contains both a Buddhist temple and Shinto shrine. The shrine has beautiful garden and a stone bridge, which were constructed by [[Toyotomi Hideyoshi]] as an offering for his mother's long life. It is next to the study attached to the Shrine offices. ==Festivals== *{{nihongo|'''Korei-taisai'''|古例大祭}}: Most important festival held on April 23. Also known as {{nihongo|'''Taga-matsuri'''|多賀まつり}}. *{{nihongo|'''Otaue-sai'''|御田植祭}}: A festival of planting rice with traditional rites held on June 7. *{{nihongo|'''Mantō-sai'''|万灯祭}}: A lantern festival held on the night of August 3-5. ==Specialty goods== *{{nihongo|'''Otaga-shakushi'''|お多賀杓子}}: A talisman shakushi (Japanese [[Ladle (spoon)|ladle]]) from the reign of [[Empress Genshō]]. Allegedly the origin of the Japanese word ''[[tadpole|otamajakushi]]''. *{{nihongo|'''Itokiri-mochi'''|糸切餅}}: A ''[[wagashi|Japanese sweet]]'' made of [[mochi]] rice sold at souvenir shops around the shrine. Itokiri-mochi was first made to celebrate the [[Mongol invasions of Japan|Japanese victory over the Mongols]].{{fact}} ==Access== About 20 minutes walk from [[Taga Taisha-mae Station]] on the [[Ohmi Railway]] [[Ohmi Railway Taga Line|Taga Line]], or about 10 minutes by bus from [[Minami-Hikone Station]] on the [[West Japan Railway Company|JR West]] [[Biwako Line]]. {{coord|35|22|54|N|136|29|10|E|region:JP_scale:30000_source:jawiki|display=title}} ==See also== *[[Takamiya-juku]], a nearby [[69 Stations of the Nakasendō|station]] on the [[Nakasendō]]. ==External links== * [http://www.tagataisya.or.jp/ Official website] {{ja icon}} [[Category:Shinto shrines in Shiga Prefecture]] {{reli-struct-stub}} {{Shinto-stub}} [[ja:多賀大社]]

It is next to the study attached to the Shrine offices. ==Festivals== *{{nihongo|'''Korei-taisai'''|古例大祭}}: Most important festival held on April 23. Also known as {{nihongo|'''Taga-matsuri'''|多賀まつり}}. *{{nihongo|'''Otaue-sai'''|御田植祭}}: A festival of planting rice with traditional rites held on June 7. *{{nihongo|'''Mantō-sai'''|万灯祭}}: A lantern festival held on the night of August 3-5. ==Specialty goods== *{{nihongo|'''Otaga-shakushi'''|お多賀杓子}}: A talisman shakushi (Japanese [[Ladle (spoon)|ladle]]) from the reign of [[Empress Genshō]]. Allegedly the origin of the Japanese word ''[[tadpole|otamajakushi]]''. *{{nihongo|'''Itokiri-mochi'''|糸切餅}}: A ''[[wagashi|Japanese sweet]]'' made of [[mochi]] rice sold at souvenir shops around the shrine. Itokiri-mochi was first made to celebrate the [[Mongol invasions of Japan|Japanese victory over the Mongols]].{{Citation needed|date=November 2009}} ==Access== About 20 minutes walk from [[Taga Taisha-mae Station]] on the [[Ohmi Railway]] [[Ohmi Railway Taga Line|Taga Line]], or about 10 minutes by bus from [[Minami-Hikone Station]] on the [[West Japan Railway Company|JR West]] [[Biwako Line]]. {{Coord|35|22|54|N|136|29|10|E|region:JP_scale:30000_source:jawiki|display=title}} ==See also== *[[Takamiya-juku]], a nearby [[69 Stations of the Nakasendō|station]] on the [[Nakasendō]]. ==External links== * [http://www.tagataisya.or.jp/ Official website] {{ja icon}} [[Category:Shinto shrines in Shiga Prefecture]] [[Category:Places of Scenic Beauty]] [[Category:Taga, Shiga]] {{Japan-struct-stub}} {{Buddhism-monastery-stub}} {{reli-struct-stub}} {{Shinto-stub}} {{Shinto shrine}} [[ja:多賀大社]] [[zh:多賀大社]]

Also known as {{nihongo|'''Taga-matsuri'''|多賀まつり}}. *{{nihongo|'''Otaue-sai'''|御田植祭}}: A festival of planting rice with traditional rites held on June 7. *{{nihongo|'''Mantō-sai'''|万灯祭}}: A lantern festival held on the night of August 3-5. ==Specialty goods== *{{nihongo|'''Otaga-shakushi'''|お多賀杓子}}: A talisman shakushi (Japanese [[Ladle (spoon)|ladle]]) from the reign of [[Empress Genshō]]. Allegedly the origin of the Japanese word ''[[tadpole|otamajakushi]]''. *{{nihongo|'''Itokiri-mochi'''|糸切餅}}: A ''[[wagashi|Japanese sweet]]'' made of [[mochi]] rice sold at souvenir shops around the shrine. Itokiri-mochi was first made to celebrate the [[Mongol invasions of Japan|Japanese victory over the Mongols]].{{Citation needed|date=November 2009}} ==Access== About 20 minutes walk from [[Taga Taisha-mae Station]] on the [[Ohmi Railway]] [[Ohmi Railway Taga Line|Taga Line]], or about 10 minutes by bus from [[Minami-Hikone Station]] on the [[West Japan Railway Company|JR West]] [[Biwako Line]]. {{Coord|35|22|54|N|136|29|10|E|region:JP_scale:30000_source:jawiki|display=title}} ==See also== *[[Takamiya-juku]], a nearby [[69 Stations of the Nakasendō|station]] on the [[Nakasendō]]. ==References== {{Reflist}} ==External links== {{Commons category|Taga-taisha}} * [http://www.tagataisya.or.jp/ Official website] {{ja icon}} [[Category:Shinto shrines in Shiga Prefecture]] [[Category:Places of Scenic Beauty]] [[Category:Taga, Shiga]] {{Japan-struct-stub}} {{Buddhism-monastery-stub}} {{reli-struct-stub}} {{Shinto-stub}} {{Shinto shrine}}

According to that text it dates from 620, having been built during a time when [[Buddhism]] and Shinto were both doing very well. It contains both a Buddhist temple and Shinto shrine. The shrine has beautiful garden and a stone bridge, which were constructed by [[Toyotomi Hideyoshi]] as an offering for his mother's long life. It is next to the study attached to the Shrine offices. ==Festivals== *{{nihongo|'''Korei-taisai'''|古例大祭}}: Most important festival held on April 22. Also known as {{nihongo|'''Taga-matsuri'''|多賀まつり}}. *{{nihongo|'''Otaue-sai'''|御田植祭}}: A festival of planting rice with traditional rites held on June 7. *{{nihongo|'''Mantō-sai'''|万灯祭}}: A lantern festival held on the night of August 3-5. ==Specialty goods== *{{nihongo|'''Otaga-shakushi'''|お多賀杓子}}: A talisman shakushi (Japanese [[Ladle (spoon)|ladle]]) from the reign of [[Empress Genshō]]. Allegedly the origin of the Japanese word ''[[tadpole|otamajakushi]]''. *{{nihongo|'''Itokiri-mochi'''|糸切餅}}: A ''[[wagashi|Japanese sweet]]'' made of [[mochi]] rice sold at souvenir shops around the shrine. Itokiri-mochi was first made to celebrate the [[Mongol invasions of Japan|Japanese victory over the Mongols]].{{Citation needed|date=November 2009}} ==Access== About 20 minutes walk from [[Taga Taisha-mae Station]] on the [[Ohmi Railway]] [[Ohmi Railway Taga Line|Taga Line]], or about 10 minutes by bus from [[Minami-Hikone Station]] on the [[West Japan Railway Company|JR West]] [[Biwako Line]]. ==See also== *[[Takamiya-juku]], a nearby [[69 Stations of the Nakasendō|station]] on the [[Nakasendō]]. ==References== {{Reflist}} ==External links== {{Commons category|Taga-taisha}} * [http://www.tagataisya.or.jp/ Official website] {{ja icon}} [[Category:Shinto shrines in Shiga Prefecture]] [[Category:Places of Scenic Beauty]] {{Japan-religious-struct-stub}} {{Buddhism-monastery-stub}} {{Shinto-stub}} {{Shinto shrine}}

Itokiri-mochi was first made to celebrate the [[Mongol invasions of Japan|Japanese victory over the Mongols]].{{Citation needed|date=November 2009}} ==Access== About 20 minutes walk from [[Taga Taisha-mae Station]] on the [[Ohmi Railway]] [[Ohmi Railway Taga Line|Taga Line]], or about 10 minutes by bus from [[Minami-Hikone Station]] on the [[West Japan Railway Company|JR West]] [[Biwako Line]]. ==See also== *[[Takamiya-juku]], a nearby [[69 Stations of the Nakasendō|station]] on the [[Nakasendō]]. ==References== {{Reflist}} ==External links== {{Commons category|Taga-taisha}} * [http://www.tagataisya.or.jp/ Official website] {{in lang|ja}} {{Shinto shrine}} [[Category:Shinto shrines in Shiga Prefecture]] [[Category:Places of Scenic Beauty]] {{Japan-religious-struct-stub}} {{Buddhism-monastery-stub}} {{Shinto-stub}}

{{Use dmy dates|date=July 2019}} {{more citations needed|date=February 2012}} {{Infobox religious building | name = Taga-taisha
多賀大社 | image = Taga taisha1.JPG | caption = ''[[Honden]]'' of Taga-taisha | map_type = Japan | map_alt = | coordinates = {{Coord|35|13|27|N|136|17|26|E|region:JP_scale:30000_source:jawiki|display=title,inline}} | map_relief = | map_size = | map_caption = | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | type = | deity = [[Izanagi]], [[Izanami]] | founded_by = | established = 660 | date_destroyed = | location = Taga, [[Taga, Shiga|Taga-chō]], [[Inukami District, Shiga|Inukami-gun]], [[Shiga Prefecture|Shiga-ken]]
522-0341 | website = {{URL|http://www.tagataisya.or.jp/}} | architecture_style = | festival = | leadership = }} [[File:Taga-taisha Haiden-b.jpg|thumb|Haiden]] {{nihongo|'''Taga-taisha'''|多賀大社}} is a [[Shinto]] [[Jinja (Shinto)|shrine]] located in [[Taga, Shiga|Taga]], [[Shiga Prefecture]], [[Japan]] dedicated to [[Izanagi]] and [[Izanami]]. It is associated with long life, successful marriage and good fortune. The shrine is frequently referred to as {{nihongo|O-taga-san|お多賀さん}} by local residents. The gardens of the inner ''[[shoin]]'' are a [[Cultural Properties of Japan|nationally designated]] [[Monuments of Japan|Place of Scenic Beauty]].{{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/401/1575 |archive-url=https://archive.is/20120803171529/https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/401/1575 |url-status=dead |archive-date=3 August 2012 |title=多賀神社奥書院庭園 |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=10 February 2012 }} ==History== This ancient shrine is old enough to have been mentioned in the ''[[Kojiki]]''. According to that text it dates from 620, having been built during a time when [[Buddhism]] and Shinto were both doing very well. It contains both a Buddhist temple and Shinto shrine. The shrine has beautiful garden and a stone bridge, which were constructed by [[Toyotomi Hideyoshi]] as an offering for his mother's long life. It is next to the study attached to the Shrine offices. ==Festivals== *{{nihongo|'''Korei-taisai'''|古例大祭}}: Most important festival held on April 22. Also known as {{nihongo|'''Taga-matsuri'''|多賀まつり}}. *{{nihongo|'''Otaue-sai'''|御田植祭}}: A festival of planting rice with traditional rites held on June 7. *{{nihongo|'''Mantō-sai'''|万灯祭}}: A lantern festival held on the night of August 3–5. ==Specialty goods== *{{nihongo|'''Otaga-shakushi'''|お多賀杓子}}: A talisman shakushi (Japanese [[Ladle (spoon)|ladle]]) from the reign of [[Empress Genshō]]. Allegedly the origin of the Japanese word ''[[tadpole|otamajakushi]]''. *{{nihongo|'''Itokiri-mochi'''|糸切餅}}: A ''[[wagashi|Japanese sweet]]'' made of [[mochi]] rice sold at souvenir shops around the shrine. Itokiri-mochi was first made to celebrate the [[Mongol invasions of Japan|Japanese victory over the Mongols]].{{Citation needed|date=November 2009}} ==Access== About 20 minutes walk from [[Taga Taisha-mae Station]] on the [[Ohmi Railway]] [[Ohmi Railway Taga Line|Taga Line]], or about 10 minutes by bus from [[Minami-Hikone Station]] on the [[West Japan Railway Company|JR West]] [[Biwako Line]]. ==See also== *[[Takamiya-juku]], a nearby [[69 Stations of the Nakasendō|station]] on the [[Nakasendō]]. ==References== {{Reflist}} ==External links== {{Commons category|Taga-taisha}} * [http://www.tagataisya.or.jp/ Official website] {{in lang|ja}} {{Shinto shrine}} {{Authority control}} [[Category:Shinto shrines in Shiga Prefecture]] [[Category:Places of Scenic Beauty]] {{Japan-religious-struct-stub}} {{Buddhism-monastery-stub}} {{Shinto-stub}}

According to that text it dates from 620, having been built during a time when [[Buddhism]] and Shinto were both doing very well. It contains both a Buddhist temple and Shinto shrine. The shrine has beautiful garden and a stone bridge, which were constructed by [[Toyotomi Hideyoshi]] as an offering for his mother's long life. Itokiri-mochi was first made to celebrate the [[Mongol invasions of Japan|Japanese victory over the Mongols]].{{Citation needed|date=November 2009}} ==Access== About 20 minutes walk from [[Taga Taisha-mae Station]] on the [[Ohmi Railway]] [[Ohmi Railway Taga Line|Taga Line]], or about 10 minutes by bus from [[Minami-Hikone Station]] on the [[West Japan Railway Company|JR West]] [[Biwako Line]]. ==See also== *[[Takamiya-juku]], a nearby [[69 Stations of the Nakasendō|station]] on the [[Nakasendō]]. ==References== {{Reflist}} ==External links== {{Commons category|Taga-taisha}} * [http://www.tagataisya.or.jp/ Official website] {{in lang|ja}} {{Shinto shrine}} {{Authority control}} [[Category:Shinto shrines in Shiga Prefecture]] [[Category:Places of Scenic Beauty]] {{Japan-religious-struct-stub}} {{Buddhism-monastery-stub}} {{Shinto-stub}}

{{Use dmy dates|date=July 2019}} {{Infobox religious building | name = Taga-taisha
多賀大社 | image = Taga taisha1.JPG | caption = ''[[Honden]]'' of Taga-taisha | map_type = Japan Shiga Prefecture#Japan | map_alt = | coordinates = {{Coord|35|13|27|N|136|17|26|E|region:JP_scale:30000_source:jawiki|display=title,inline}} | map_relief = 1 | map_size = | map_caption = | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | type = | deity = [[Izanagi]], [[Izanami]] | founded_by = | established = pre-[[Nara period]] | date_destroyed = | location = 604 Taga, Inukami-gun, Shiga-ken 522-0341 | website = {{URL|http://www.tagataisya.or.jp/}} | architecture_style = | festival = | footnotes ={{box|background=white|align=center|wide=yes|border size=3px|border color=green|text align=center|[[Monuments of Japan|National Palace of Scenic Beauty]]}} }} [[File:Taga-taisha Haiden-b.jpg|thumb|280px|Haiden]] {{nihongo|'''Taga-taisha'''|多賀大社}} is a [[Shinto]] [[Jinja (Shinto)|shrine]] located in the town of [[Taga, Shiga|Taga]], [[Inukami District, Shiga|Inukami District]], [[Shiga Prefecture]], [[Japan]. The shrine is frequently referred to as {{nihongo|O-Taga-san|お多賀さん}} by local residents.{{cite web |title=Taga Taisha official home page |url=http://www.tagataisya.or.jp/about/ |website=About Us |publisher=Taga Taisha}}{{in lang|ja}} The gardens of the inner ''[[shoin]]'', which date to the [[Momoyama period]] are a [[Cultural Properties of Japan|nationally designated]] [[Monuments of Japan|Place of Scenic Beauty]],{{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/401/1575 |archive-url=https://archive.is/20120803171529/https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/401/1575 |url-status=dead |archive-date=3 August 2012 |title=多賀神社奥書院庭園 |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |access-date=10 February 2012 }}{{Cite web|title=THE 10 BEST Things to Do Near Taga Taisha, Taga-cho - Tripadvisor|url=https://www.tripadvisor.com/AttractionsNear-g1121270-d1992498-Taga_Taisha-Taga_cho_Inukami_gun_Shiga_Prefecture_Kinki.html|access-date=2021-06-05|website=www.tripadvisor.com|language=en}}whereas the mid-[[Edo period]] ''Shoin'' itself is a Shiga Prefectural Important Cultural Property. ==Main ''kami''== * [[Izanagi]] * [[Izanami]] ==History== The foundation of Taga Taisha is unknown. It is mentioned in the ancient ''[[Kojiki]]'' and ''[[Nihon Shoki]]'' chronicles from 712 AD. The shrine's legend connects it with the legendary hero [[Yamato Takeru]], whose son, [[Inugami no Mitasuki]] was sent as an emissary to [[Japanese missions to Sui China|Sui China]] in 614 AD and the first [[Japanese missions to Tang China|Japanese embassy to Tang China]] in 630 AD. He was also the ancestor of the Inukami clan, from which [[Inukami District, Shiga]] (where the shrine is located) takes its name. However, in the 914 AD ''[[Engishiki]]'' records, it is listed only as a small shrine. From the [[Heian period]] it was revered as having efficiency for childbirth, vocational aid, fire extinguishing and longevity. During then [[Muromachi period]], as pilgrimages to the [[Ise Grand Shrine]] and the [[Kumano shrine|Kumano Sanzan]] gained in popularity, pilgrims were encouraged to visit Taga Shrine as part of the route. In the [[Sengoku period]], [[Toyotomi Hideyoshi]] had a strong faith in this shrine and frequently prayed for an extension of his mother's life. He donated 10,000 ''[[koku]]'', or the equivalent revenue of one year for a ''[[daimyō]]'' for its reconstruction and upkeep. The shrine was destroyed by a fire in 1615, but was rebuilt immediately by [[Shogun]] [[Tokugawa Hidetada]] and reconstructed in 1633 by [[Tokugawa Iemitsu]]. In 1651, [[Ii Naotaka]] of [[Hikone Domain]] granted it estates of 150 ''[[koku]]'' for its upkeep. However, much of the shrine was destroyed again by fire in 1733, and by a storm in 1791. Each time, it was rebuilt with the assistance of the [[Tokugawa shogunate]] and Hikone Domain. With the establishment of [[State Shinto]] following the [[Meiji restoration]], the shrine was designated a prefectural shrine under the [[Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines]] in 1871. It was promoted to a {{nihongo3|Imperial shrine, 2nd rank|官幣中社|Kanpei-chūsha}} in 1885 and to a {{nihongo3|Imperial shrine, 1st rank|官幣大社|Kanpei-taisha}} in 1914. Many of the shrine's buildings are Registered Tangible Cultural Properties of Taga Town.{{cite web |title=Registered Cultural Properties |url=https://www.town.taga.lg.jp/akebono/bunkazai/shitei.html |website=Taga Town Cultural Properties Center |publisher=Taga Town}}{{in lang|ja}} ==Festivals== *{{nihongo|'''Korei-taisai'''|古例大祭}}: Most important festival held on April 22. Also known as {{nihongo|'''Taga-matsuri'''|多賀まつり}}. *{{nihongo|'''Otaue-sai'''|御田植祭}}: A festival of planting rice with traditional rites held on June 7. *{{nihongo|'''Mantō-sai'''|万灯祭}}: A lantern festival held on the night of August 3–5. ==Specialty goods== *{{nihongo|'''Otaga-shakushi'''|お多賀杓子}}: A talisman ''shakushi'' (Japanese [[Ladle (spoon)|ladle]]) from the reign of [[Empress Genshō]]. Allegedly the origin of the Japanese word ''[[tadpole|otamajakushi]]''. *{{nihongo|'''Itokiri-mochi'''|糸切餅}}: A ''[[wagashi|Japanese sweet]]'' made of ''[[mochi]]'' sticky rice sold at souvenir shops around the shrine. ''Itokiri-mochi'' was first made to celebrate the [[Mongol invasions of Japan|Japanese victory over the Mongols]].{{Citation needed|date=November 2009}} ==Access== The shrine is about a 20 minute walk from [[Taga Taisha-mae Station]] on the [[Ohmi Railway]] [[Ohmi Railway Taga Line|Taga Line]], or about ten minutes by bus from [[Minami-Hikone Station]] on the [[West Japan Railway Company|JR West]] [[Biwako Line]]. ==See also== *[[Takamiya-juku]], the closest of the [[69 Stations of the Nakasendō]] to the shrine ==References== {{Reflist}} ==External links== {{Commons category|Taga-taisha}} * [http://www.tagataisya.or.jp/ Official website] {{in lang|ja}} {{Shinto shrine}} {{Authority control}} [[Category:Shinto shrines in Shiga Prefecture]] [[Category:Places of Scenic Beauty]] [[Category:Taga, Shiga]] [[Category:Ōmi Province]]

Also known as {{nihongo|'''Taga-matsuri'''|多賀まつり}}. *{{nihongo|'''Otaue-sai'''|御田植祭}}: A festival of planting rice with traditional rites held on June 7. *{{nihongo|'''Mantō-sai'''|万灯祭}}: A lantern festival held on the night of August 3–5. ==Specialty goods== *{{nihongo|'''Otaga-shakushi'''|お多賀杓子}}: A talisman ''shakushi'' (Japanese [[Ladle (spoon)|ladle]]) from the reign of [[Empress Genshō]]. Allegedly the origin of the Japanese word ''[[tadpole|otamajakushi]]''. *{{nihongo|'''Itokiri-mochi'''|糸切餅}}: A ''[[wagashi|Japanese sweet]]'' made of ''[[mochi]]'' sticky rice sold at souvenir shops around the shrine. ''Itokiri-mochi'' was first made to celebrate the [[Mongol invasions of Japan|Japanese victory over the Mongols]].{{Citation needed|date=November 2009}} ==Access== The shrine is about a 20 minute walk from [[Taga Taisha-mae Station]] on the [[Ohmi Railway]] [[Ohmi Railway Taga Line|Taga Line]], or about ten minutes by bus from [[Minami-Hikone Station]] on the [[West Japan Railway Company|JR West]] [[Biwako Line]]. ==See also== *[[Takamiya-juku]], the closest of the [[69 Stations of the Nakasendō]] to the shrine ==References== {{Reflist}} ==External links== {{Commons category|Taga-taisha}} * [http://www.tagataisya.or.jp/ Official website] {{in lang|ja}} {{Shinto shrine}} {{Authority control}} [[Category:Shinto shrines in Shiga Prefecture]] [[Category:Places of Scenic Beauty]] [[Category:Taga, Shiga]] [[Category:Ōmi Province]]

{{Use dmy dates|date=July 2019}} {{Infobox religious building | name = Taga-taisha
多賀大社 | image = Taga taisha1.JPG | caption = ''[[Honden]]'' of Taga-taisha | map_type = Japan Shiga Prefecture#Japan | map_alt = | coordinates = {{Coord|35|13|27|N|136|17|26|E|region:JP_scale:30000_source:jawiki|display=title,inline}} | map_relief = 1 | map_size = | map_caption = | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | type = | deity = [[Izanagi]], [[Izanami]] | founded_by = | established = pre-[[Nara period]] | date_destroyed = | location = 604 Taga, Inukami-gun, Shiga-ken 522-0341 | website = {{URL|http://www.tagataisya.or.jp/}} | architecture_style = | festival = | footnotes ={{box|background=white|align=center|wide=yes|border size=3px|border color=green|text align=center|[[Monuments of Japan|National Palace of Scenic Beauty]]}} }} [[File:Taga-taisha Haiden-b.jpg|thumb|280px|Haiden]] {{nihongo|'''Taga-taisha'''|多賀大社}} is a [[Shinto]] [[Jinja (Shinto)|shrine]] located in the town of [[Taga, Shiga|Taga]], [[Inukami District, Shiga|Inukami District]], [[Shiga Prefecture]], [[Japan]]. The shrine is frequently referred to as {{nihongo|O-Taga-san|お多賀さん}} by local residents.{{cite web |title=Taga Taisha official home page |url=http://www.tagataisya.or.jp/about/ |website=About Us |publisher=Taga Taisha}}{{in lang|ja}} The gardens of the inner ''[[shoin]]'', which date to the [[Momoyama period]] are a [[Cultural Properties of Japan|nationally designated]] [[Monuments of Japan|Place of Scenic Beauty]],{{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/401/1575 |archive-url=https://archive.is/20120803171529/https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/401/1575 |url-status=dead |archive-date=3 August 2012 |title=多賀神社奥書院庭園 |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |access-date=10 February 2012 }}{{Cite web|title=THE 10 BEST Things to Do Near Taga Taisha, Taga-cho - Tripadvisor|url=https://www.tripadvisor.com/AttractionsNear-g1121270-d1992498-Taga_Taisha-Taga_cho_Inukami_gun_Shiga_Prefecture_Kinki.html|access-date=2021-06-05|website=www.tripadvisor.com|language=en}} whereas the mid-[[Edo period]] ''Shoin'' itself is a Shiga Prefectural Important Cultural Property. ==Main ''kami''== * [[Izanagi]] * [[Izanami]] ==History== The foundation of Taga Taisha is unknown. It is mentioned in the ancient ''[[Kojiki]]'' and ''[[Nihon Shoki]]'' chronicles from 712 AD. The shrine's legend connects it with the legendary hero [[Yamato Takeru]], whose son, [[Inugami no Mitasuki]] was sent as an emissary to [[Japanese missions to Sui China|Sui China]] in 614 AD and the first [[Japanese missions to Tang China|Japanese embassy to Tang China]] in 630 AD. He was also the ancestor of the Inukami clan, from which [[Inukami District, Shiga]] (where the shrine is located) takes its name. However, in the 914 AD ''[[Engishiki]]'' records, it is listed only as a small shrine. From the [[Heian period]] it was revered as having efficiency for childbirth, vocational aid, fire extinguishing and longevity. During then [[Muromachi period]], as pilgrimages to the [[Ise Grand Shrine]] and the [[Kumano shrine|Kumano Sanzan]] gained in popularity, pilgrims were encouraged to visit Taga Shrine as part of the route. In the [[Sengoku period]], [[Toyotomi Hideyoshi]] had a strong faith in this shrine and frequently prayed for an extension of his mother's life. He donated 10,000 ''[[koku]]'', or the equivalent revenue of one year for a ''[[daimyō]]'' for its reconstruction and upkeep. The shrine was destroyed by a fire in 1615, but was rebuilt immediately by [[Shogun]] [[Tokugawa Hidetada]] and reconstructed in 1633 by [[Tokugawa Iemitsu]]. In 1651, [[Ii Naotaka]] of [[Hikone Domain]] granted it estates of 150 ''[[koku]]'' for its upkeep. However, much of the shrine was destroyed again by fire in 1733, and by a storm in 1791. Each time, it was rebuilt with the assistance of the [[Tokugawa shogunate]] and Hikone Domain. With the establishment of [[State Shinto]] following the [[Meiji restoration]], the shrine was designated a prefectural shrine under the [[Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines]] in 1871. It was promoted to a {{nihongo3|Imperial shrine, 2nd rank|官幣中社|Kanpei-chūsha}} in 1885 and to a {{nihongo3|Imperial shrine, 1st rank|官幣大社|Kanpei-taisha}} in 1914. Many of the shrine's buildings are Registered Tangible Cultural Properties of Taga Town.{{cite web |title=Registered Cultural Properties |url=https://www.town.taga.lg.jp/akebono/bunkazai/shitei.html |website=Taga Town Cultural Properties Center |publisher=Taga Town}}{{in lang|ja}} ==Festivals== *{{nihongo|'''Korei-taisai'''|古例大祭}}: Most important festival held on April 22. Also known as {{nihongo|'''Taga-matsuri'''|多賀まつり}}. *{{nihongo|'''Otaue-sai'''|御田植祭}}: A festival of planting rice with traditional rites held on June 7. *{{nihongo|'''Mantō-sai'''|万灯祭}}: A lantern festival held on the night of August 3–5. ==Specialty goods== *{{nihongo|'''Otaga-shakushi'''|お多賀杓子}}: A talisman ''shakushi'' (Japanese [[Ladle (spoon)|ladle]]) from the reign of [[Empress Genshō]]. Allegedly the origin of the Japanese word ''[[tadpole|otamajakushi]]''. *{{nihongo|'''Itokiri-mochi'''|糸切餅}}: A ''[[wagashi|Japanese sweet]]'' made of ''[[mochi]]'' sticky rice sold at souvenir shops around the shrine. ''Itokiri-mochi'' was first made to celebrate the [[Mongol invasions of Japan|Japanese victory over the Mongols]].{{Citation needed|date=November 2009}} ==Access== The shrine is about a 20 minute walk from [[Taga Taisha-mae Station]] on the [[Ohmi Railway]] [[Ohmi Railway Taga Line|Taga Line]], or about ten minutes by bus from [[Minami-Hikone Station]] on the [[West Japan Railway Company|JR West]] [[Biwako Line]]. ==See also== *[[Takamiya-juku]], the closest of the [[69 Stations of the Nakasendō]] to the shrine ==References== {{Reflist}} ==External links== {{Commons category|Taga-taisha}} * [http://www.tagataisya.or.jp/ Official website] {{in lang|ja}} {{Shinto shrine}} {{Authority control}} [[Category:Shinto shrines in Shiga Prefecture]] [[Category:Places of Scenic Beauty]] [[Category:Taga, Shiga]] [[Category:Ōmi Province]]

Also known as {{nihongo|'''Taga-matsuri'''|多賀まつり}}. *{{nihongo|'''Otaue-sai'''|御田植祭}}: A festival of planting rice with traditional rites held on June 7. *{{nihongo|'''Mantō-sai'''|万灯祭}}: A lantern festival held on the night of August 3–5. ==Specialty goods== *{{nihongo|'''Otaga-shakushi'''|お多賀杓子}}: A talisman ''shakushi'' (Japanese [[Ladle (spoon)|ladle]]) from the reign of [[Empress Genshō]]. Allegedly the origin of the Japanese word ''[[tadpole|otamajakushi]]''. *{{nihongo|'''Itokiri-mochi'''|糸切餅}}: A ''[[wagashi|Japanese sweet]]'' made of ''[[mochi]]'' sticky rice sold at souvenir shops around the shrine. ''Itokiri-mochi'' was first made to celebrate the [[Mongol invasions of Japan|Japanese victory over the Mongols]].{{Citation needed|date=November 2009}} ==Access== The shrine is about a 20 minute walk from [[Taga Taisha-mae Station]] on the [[Ohmi Railway]] [[Ohmi Railway Taga Line|Taga Line]], or about ten minutes by bus from [[Minami-Hikone Station]] on the [[West Japan Railway Company|JR West]] [[Biwako Line]]. ==See also== *[[Takamiya-juku]], the closest of the [[69 Stations of the Nakasendō]] to the shrine ==References== {{Reflist}} ==External links== {{Commons category|Taga-taisha}} * [http://www.tagataisya.or.jp/ Official website] {{in lang|ja}} {{Shinto shrine}} {{Authority control}} [[Category:Shinto shrines in Shiga Prefecture]] [[Category:Places of Scenic Beauty]] [[Category:Taga, Shiga]] [[Category:Ōmi Province]]

{{Use dmy dates|date=July 2019}} {{Infobox religious building | name = Taga-taisha
多賀大社 | image = Taga taisha1.JPG | caption = ''[[Honden]]'' of Taga-taisha | map_type = Japan Shiga Prefecture#Japan | map_alt = | coordinates = {{Coord|35|13|27|N|136|17|26|E|region:JP_scale:30000_source:jawiki|display=title,inline}} | map_relief = 1 | map_size = | map_caption = | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | type = | deity = [[Izanagi]], [[Izanami]] | founded_by = | established = pre-[[Nara period]] | date_destroyed = | location = 604 Taga, Inukami-gun, Shiga-ken 522-0341 | website = {{URL|http://www.tagataisya.or.jp/}} | architecture_style = | festival = | footnotes ={{box|background=white|align=center|wide=yes|border size=3px|border color=green|text align=center|[[Monuments of Japan|National Palace of Scenic Beauty]]}} }} [[File:Taga-taisha Haiden-b.jpg|thumb|280px|Haiden]] {{nihongo|'''Taga-taisha'''|多賀大社}} is a [[Shinto]] [[Jinja (Shinto)|shrine]] located in the town of [[Taga, Shiga|Taga]], [[Inukami District, Shiga|Inukami District]], [[Shiga Prefecture]], [[Japan]]. The shrine is frequently referred to as {{nihongo|O-Taga-san|お多賀さん}} by local residents.{{cite web |title=Taga Taisha official home page |url=http://www.tagataisya.or.jp/about/ |website=About Us |publisher=Taga Taisha}}{{in lang|ja}} The gardens of the inner ''[[shoin]]'', which date to the [[Momoyama period]] are a [[Cultural Properties of Japan|nationally designated]] [[Monuments of Japan|Place of Scenic Beauty]],{{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/401/1575 |archive-url=https://archive.today/20120803171529/https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/401/1575 |url-status=dead |archive-date=3 August 2012 |title=多賀神社奥書院庭園 |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |access-date=10 February 2012 }}{{Cite web|title=THE 10 BEST Things to Do Near Taga Taisha, Taga-cho - Tripadvisor|url=https://www.tripadvisor.com/AttractionsNear-g1121270-d1992498-Taga_Taisha-Taga_cho_Inukami_gun_Shiga_Prefecture_Kinki.html|access-date=2021-06-05|website=www.tripadvisor.com|language=en}} whereas the mid-[[Edo period]] ''Shoin'' itself is a Shiga Prefectural Important Cultural Property. ==Main ''kami''== * [[Izanagi]] * [[Izanami]] [[Category:Beppyo shrines]] ==History== The foundation of Taga Taisha is unknown. It is mentioned in the ancient ''[[Kojiki]]'' and ''[[Nihon Shoki]]'' chronicles from 712 AD. The shrine's legend connects it with the legendary hero [[Yamato Takeru]], whose son, [[Inugami no Mitasuki]] was sent as an emissary to [[Japanese missions to Sui China|Sui China]] in 614 AD and the first [[Japanese missions to Tang China|Japanese embassy to Tang China]] in 630 AD. He was also the ancestor of the Inukami clan, from which [[Inukami District, Shiga]] (where the shrine is located) takes its name. However, in the 914 AD ''[[Engishiki]]'' records, it is listed only as a small shrine. From the [[Heian period]] it was revered as having efficiency for childbirth, vocational aid, fire extinguishing and longevity. During then [[Muromachi period]], as pilgrimages to the [[Ise Grand Shrine]] and the [[Kumano shrine|Kumano Sanzan]] gained in popularity, pilgrims were encouraged to visit Taga Shrine as part of the route. In the [[Sengoku period]], [[Toyotomi Hideyoshi]] had a strong faith in this shrine and frequently prayed for an extension of his mother's life. He donated 10,000 ''[[koku]]'', or the equivalent revenue of one year for a ''[[daimyō]]'' for its reconstruction and upkeep. The shrine was destroyed by a fire in 1615, but was rebuilt immediately by [[Shogun]] [[Tokugawa Hidetada]] and reconstructed in 1633 by [[Tokugawa Iemitsu]]. In 1651, [[Ii Naotaka]] of [[Hikone Domain]] granted it estates of 150 ''[[koku]]'' for its upkeep. However, much of the shrine was destroyed again by fire in 1733, and by a storm in 1791. Each time, it was rebuilt with the assistance of the [[Tokugawa shogunate]] and Hikone Domain. With the establishment of [[State Shinto]] following the [[Meiji restoration]], the shrine was designated a prefectural shrine under the [[Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines]] in 1871. It was promoted to a {{nihongo3|Imperial shrine, 2nd rank|官幣中社|Kanpei-chūsha}} in 1885 and to a {{nihongo3|Imperial shrine, 1st rank|官幣大社|Kanpei-taisha}} in 1914. Many of the shrine's buildings are Registered Tangible Cultural Properties of Taga Town.{{cite web |title=Registered Cultural Properties |url=https://www.town.taga.lg.jp/akebono/bunkazai/shitei.html |website=Taga Town Cultural Properties Center |publisher=Taga Town}}{{in lang|ja}} ==Festivals== *{{nihongo|'''Korei-taisai'''|古例大祭}}: Most important festival held on April 22. Also known as {{nihongo|'''Taga-matsuri'''|多賀まつり}}. *{{nihongo|'''Otaue-sai'''|御田植祭}}: A festival of planting rice with traditional rites held on June 7. *{{nihongo|'''Mantō-sai'''|万灯祭}}: A lantern festival held on the night of August 3–5. ==Specialty goods== *{{nihongo|'''Otaga-shakushi'''|お多賀杓子}}: A talisman ''shakushi'' (Japanese [[Ladle (spoon)|ladle]]) from the reign of [[Empress Genshō]]. Allegedly the origin of the Japanese word ''[[tadpole|otamajakushi]]''. *{{nihongo|'''Itokiri-mochi'''|糸切餅}}: A ''[[wagashi|Japanese sweet]]'' made of ''[[mochi]]'' sticky rice sold at souvenir shops around the shrine. ''Itokiri-mochi'' was first made to celebrate the [[Mongol invasions of Japan|Japanese victory over the Mongols]].{{Citation needed|date=November 2009}} ==Access== The shrine is about a 20 minute walk from [[Taga Taisha-mae Station]] on the [[Ohmi Railway]] [[Ohmi Railway Taga Line|Taga Line]], or about ten minutes by bus from [[Minami-Hikone Station]] on the [[West Japan Railway Company|JR West]] [[Biwako Line]]. ==See also== *[[Takamiya-juku]], the closest of the [[69 Stations of the Nakasendō]] to the shrine ==References== {{Reflist}} ==External links== {{Commons category|Taga-taisha}} * [http://www.tagataisya.or.jp/ Official website] {{in lang|ja}} {{Shinto shrine}} {{Authority control}} [[Category:Shinto shrines in Shiga Prefecture]] [[Category:Places of Scenic Beauty]] [[Category:Taga, Shiga]] [[Category:Ōmi Province]] [[Category:Kanpei-taisha]]

Many of the shrine's buildings are Registered Tangible Cultural Properties of Taga Town.{{cite web |title=Registered Cultural Properties |url=https://www.town.taga.lg.jp/akebono/bunkazai/shitei.html |website=Taga Town Cultural Properties Center |publisher=Taga Town}}{{in lang|ja}} ==Festivals== *{{nihongo|'''Korei-taisai'''|古例大祭}}: Most important festival held on April 22. Also known as {{nihongo|'''Taga-matsuri'''|多賀まつり}}. *{{nihongo|'''Otaue-sai'''|御田植祭}}: A festival of planting rice with traditional rites held on June 7. *{{nihongo|'''Mantō-sai'''|万灯祭}}: A lantern festival held on the night of August 3–5. ==Specialty goods== *{{nihongo|'''Otaga-shakushi'''|お多賀杓子}}: A talisman ''shakushi'' (Japanese [[Ladle (spoon)|ladle]]) from the reign of [[Empress Genshō]]. Allegedly the origin of the Japanese word ''[[tadpole|otamajakushi]]''. *{{nihongo|'''Itokiri-mochi'''|糸切餅}}: A ''[[wagashi|Japanese sweet]]'' made of ''[[mochi]]'' sticky rice sold at souvenir shops around the shrine. ''Itokiri-mochi'' was first made to celebrate the [[Mongol invasions of Japan|Japanese victory over the Mongols]].{{Citation needed|date=November 2009}} ==Access== The shrine is about a 20 minute walk from [[Taga Taisha-mae Station]] on the [[Ohmi Railway]] [[Ohmi Railway Taga Line|Taga Line]], or about ten minutes by bus from [[Minami-Hikone Station]] on the [[West Japan Railway Company|JR West]] [[Biwako Line]]. ==See also== *[[Takamiya-juku]], the closest of the [[69 Stations of the Nakasendō]] to the shrine ==References== {{Reflist}} ==External links== {{Commons category|Taga-taisha}} * [http://www.tagataisya.or.jp/ Official website] {{in lang|ja}} {{Shinto shrine}} {{Authority control}} [[Category:Shinto shrines in Shiga Prefecture]] [[Category:Places of Scenic Beauty]] [[Category:Taga, Shiga]] [[Category:Ōmi Province]] [[Category:Kanpei Taisha]] [[Category:Sannomiya]] [[Category:Shikinai Shosha]]

多賀大社 | image = Taga taisha1.JPG | caption = ''[[Honden]]'' of Taga-taisha | map_type = Japan Shiga Prefecture#Japan | map_alt = | coordinates = {{Coord|35|13|27|N|136|17|26|E|region:JP_scale:30000_source:jawiki|display=title,inline}} | map_relief = 1 | map_size = | map_caption = | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | type = | deity = [[Izanagi]], [[Izanami]] | founded_by = | established = pre-[[Nara period]] | date_destroyed = | location = 604 Taga, Inukami-gun, Shiga-ken 522-0341 | website = {{URL|http://www.tagataisya.or.jp/}} | architecture_style = | festival = | footnotes ={{box|background=white|align=center|wide=yes|border size=3px|border color=green|text align=center|[[Monuments of Japan|National Palace of Scenic Beauty]]}} }} [[File:Taga-taisha Haiden-b.jpg|thumb|280px|Haiden]] {{nihongo|'''Taga-taisha'''|多賀大社}} is a [[Shinto]] [[Jinja (Shinto)|shrine]] located in the town of [[Taga, Shiga|Taga]], [[Inukami District, Shiga|Inukami District]], [[Shiga Prefecture]], [[Japan]]. The shrine is frequently referred to as {{nihongo|O-Taga-san|お多賀さん}} by local residents.{{cite web |title=Taga Taisha official home page |url=http://www.tagataisya.or.jp/about/ |website=About Us |publisher=Taga Taisha}}{{in lang|ja}} The gardens of the inner ''[[shoin]]'', which date to the [[Momoyama period]] are a [[Cultural Properties of Japan|nationally designated]] [[Monuments of Japan|Place of Scenic Beauty]],{{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/401/1575 |archive-url=https://archive.today/20120803171529/https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/401/1575 |url-status=dead |archive-date=3 August 2012 |title=多賀神社奥書院庭園 |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |access-date=10 February 2012 }}{{Cite web|title=THE 10 BEST Things to Do Near Taga Taisha, Taga-cho - Tripadvisor|url=https://www.tripadvisor.com/AttractionsNear-g1121270-d1992498-Taga_Taisha-Taga_cho_Inukami_gun_Shiga_Prefecture_Kinki.html|access-date=2021-06-05|website=www.tripadvisor.com|language=en}} whereas the mid-[[Edo period]] ''Shoin'' itself is a Shiga Prefectural Important Cultural Property. ==Main ''kami''== * [[Izanagi]] * [[Izanami]] [[Category:Beppyo shrines]] ==History== The foundation of Taga Taisha is unknown. It is mentioned in the ancient ''[[Kojiki]]'' and ''[[Nihon Shoki]]'' chronicles from 712 AD. The shrine's legend connects it with the legendary hero [[Yamato Takeru]], whose son, [[Inugami no Mitasuki]] was sent as an emissary to [[Japanese missions to Sui China|Sui China]] in 614 AD and the first [[Japanese missions to Tang China|Japanese embassy to Tang China]] in 630 AD. He was also the ancestor of the Inukami clan, from which [[Inukami District, Shiga]] (where the shrine is located) takes its name. However, in the 914 AD ''[[Engishiki]]'' records, it is listed only as a small shrine. From the [[Heian period]] it was revered as having efficiency for childbirth, vocational aid, fire extinguishing and longevity. During then [[Muromachi period]], as pilgrimages to the [[Ise Grand Shrine]] and the [[Kumano shrine|Kumano Sanzan]] gained in popularity, pilgrims were encouraged to visit Taga Shrine as part of the route. In the [[Sengoku period]], [[Toyotomi Hideyoshi]] had a strong faith in this shrine and frequently prayed for an extension of his mother's life. He donated 10,000 ''[[koku]]'', or the equivalent revenue of one year for a ''[[daimyō]]'' for its reconstruction and upkeep. The shrine was destroyed by a fire in 1615, but was rebuilt immediately by [[Shogun]] [[Tokugawa Hidetada]] and reconstructed in 1633 by [[Tokugawa Iemitsu]]. In 1651, [[Ii Naotaka]] of [[Hikone Domain]] granted it estates of 150 ''[[koku]]'' for its upkeep. However, much of the shrine was destroyed again by fire in 1733, and by a storm in 1791. Each time, it was rebuilt with the assistance of the [[Tokugawa shogunate]] and Hikone Domain. With the establishment of [[State Shinto]] following the [[Meiji restoration]], the shrine was designated a prefectural shrine under the [[Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines]] in 1871. It was promoted to a {{nihongo3|Imperial shrine, 2nd rank|官幣中社|[[Kanpei Chūsha]]}} in 1885 and to a {{nihongo3|Imperial shrine, 1st rank|官幣大社|Kanpei-taisha}} in 1914. Many of the shrine's buildings are Registered Tangible Cultural Properties of Taga Town.{{cite web |title=Registered Cultural Properties |url=https://www.town.taga.lg.jp/akebono/bunkazai/shitei.html |website=Taga Town Cultural Properties Center |publisher=Taga Town}}{{in lang|ja}} ==Festivals== *{{nihongo|'''Korei-taisai'''|古例大祭}}: Most important festival held on April 22. Allegedly the origin of the Japanese word ''[[tadpole|otamajakushi]]''. *{{nihongo|'''Itokiri-mochi'''|糸切餅}}: A ''[[wagashi|Japanese sweet]]'' made of ''[[mochi]]'' sticky rice sold at souvenir shops around the shrine. ''Itokiri-mochi'' was first made to celebrate the [[Mongol invasions of Japan|Japanese victory over the Mongols]].{{Citation needed|date=November 2009}} ==Access== The shrine is about a 20 minute walk from [[Taga Taisha-mae Station]] on the [[Ohmi Railway]] [[Ohmi Railway Taga Line|Taga Line]], or about ten minutes by bus from [[Minami-Hikone Station]] on the [[West Japan Railway Company|JR West]] [[Biwako Line]]. ==See also== *[[Takamiya-juku]], the closest of the [[69 Stations of the Nakasendō]] to the shrine ==References== {{Reflist}} ==External links== {{Commons category|Taga-taisha}} * [http://www.tagataisya.or.jp/ Official website] {{in lang|ja}} {{Shinto shrine}} {{Authority control}} [[Category:Shinto shrines in Shiga Prefecture]] [[Category:Places of Scenic Beauty]] [[Category:Taga, Shiga]] [[Category:Ōmi Province]] [[Category:Kanpei Taisha]] [[Category:Sannomiya]] [[Category:Shikinai Shosha]]

It was promoted to a {{nihongo3|Imperial shrine, 2nd rank|官幣中社|[[Kanpei Chūsha]]}} in 1885 and to a {{nihongo3|Imperial shrine, 1st rank|官幣大社|Kanpei-taisha}} in 1914. Many of the shrine's buildings are Registered Tangible Cultural Properties of Taga Town.{{cite web |title=Registered Cultural Properties |url=https://www.town.taga.lg.jp/akebono/bunkazai/shitei.html |website=Taga Town Cultural Properties Center |publisher=Taga Town}}{{in lang|ja}} ==Festivals== *{{nihongo|'''Korei-taisai'''|古例大祭}}: Most important festival held on April 22. Also known as {{nihongo|'''Taga-matsuri'''|多賀まつり}}. *{{nihongo|'''Otaue-sai'''|御田植祭}}: A festival of planting rice with traditional rites held on June 7. *{{nihongo|'''Mantō-sai'''|万灯祭}}: A lantern festival held on the night of August 3–5. ==Specialty goods== *{{nihongo|'''Otaga-shakushi'''|お多賀杓子}}: A talisman ''shakushi'' (Japanese [[Ladle (spoon)|ladle]]) from the reign of [[Empress Genshō]]. Allegedly the origin of the Japanese word ''[[tadpole|otamajakushi]]''. *{{nihongo|'''Itokiri-mochi'''|糸切餅}}: A ''[[wagashi|Japanese sweet]]'' made of ''[[mochi]]'' sticky rice sold at souvenir shops around the shrine. ''Itokiri-mochi'' was first made to celebrate the [[Mongol invasions of Japan|Japanese victory over the Mongols]].{{Citation needed|date=November 2009}} ==Access== The shrine is about a 20 minute walk from [[Taga Taisha-mae Station]] on the [[Ohmi Railway]] [[Ohmi Railway Taga Line|Taga Line]], or about ten minutes by bus from [[Minami-Hikone Station]] on the [[West Japan Railway Company|JR West]] [[Biwako Line]]. ==See also== *[[Takamiya-juku]], the closest of the [[69 Stations of the Nakasendō]] to the shrine ==References== {{Reflist}} ==External links== {{Commons category|Taga-taisha}} * [http://www.tagataisya.or.jp/ Official website] {{in lang|ja}} {{Shinto shrine}} {{Authority control}} [[Category:Shinto shrines in Shiga Prefecture]] [[Category:Places of Scenic Beauty]] [[Category:Taga, Shiga]] [[Category:Ōmi Province]] [[Category:Kanpei Taisha]] [[Category:Sannomiya]] [[Category:Shikinai Shosha]] [[Category:Pet shrines]] [[Category:Taisha]]

{{Use dmy dates|date=July 2019}} {{Infobox religious building | name = Taga-taisha
多賀大社 | image = Taga taisha1.JPG | caption = ''[[Honden]]'' of Taga-taisha | map_type = Japan Shiga Prefecture#Japan | coordinates = {{Coord|35|13|27|N|136|17|26|E|region:JP_scale:30000_source:jawiki|display=title,inline}} | map_relief = 1 | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | deity = [[Izanagi]], [[Izanami]] | established = Pre-[[Nara period]] | location = 604 Taga, Inukami-gun, Shiga-ken 522-0341 | website = {{URL|http://www.tagataisya.or.jp/}} | footnotes ={{box|background=white|align=center|wide=yes|border size=3px|border color=green|text align=center|[[Monuments of Japan|National Palace of Scenic Beauty]]}} }} [[File:Taga-taisha Haiden-b.jpg|thumb|280px|Haiden]] {{nihongo|'''Taga-taisha'''|多賀大社}} is a [[Shinto]] [[Jinja (Shinto)|shrine]] located in the town of [[Taga, Shiga|Taga]], [[Inukami District, Shiga|Inukami District]], [[Shiga Prefecture]], [[Japan]]. The shrine is frequently referred to as {{nihongo|O-Taga-san|お多賀さん}} by local residents.{{cite web |title=Taga Taisha official home page |url=http://www.tagataisya.or.jp/about/ |website=About Us |publisher=Taga Taisha}}{{in lang|ja}} The gardens of the inner ''[[shoin]]'', which date to the [[Momoyama period]] are a [[Cultural Properties of Japan|nationally designated]] [[Monuments of Japan|Place of Scenic Beauty]],{{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/401/1575 |archive-url=https://archive.today/20120803171529/https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/401/1575 |url-status=dead |archive-date=3 August 2012 |title=多賀神社奥書院庭園 |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |access-date=10 February 2012 }}{{Cite web|title=THE 10 BEST Things to Do Near Taga Taisha, Taga-cho - Tripadvisor|url=https://www.tripadvisor.com/AttractionsNear-g1121270-d1992498-Taga_Taisha-Taga_cho_Inukami_gun_Shiga_Prefecture_Kinki.html|access-date=2021-06-05|website=www.tripadvisor.com|language=en}} whereas the mid-[[Edo period]] ''Shoin'' itself is a Shiga Prefectural Important Cultural Property. ==Main ''kami''== * [[Izanagi]] * [[Izanami]] [[Category:Beppyo shrines]] ==History== The foundation of Taga Taisha is unknown. It is mentioned in the ancient {{Lang|ja-latn|[[Kojiki]]}} and {{Lang|ja-latn|[[Nihon Shoki]]}} chronicles from 712 AD. The shrine's legend connects it with the legendary hero [[Yamato Takeru]], whose son, [[Inugami no Mitasuki]] was sent as an emissary to [[Japanese missions to Sui China|Sui China]] in 614 AD and the first [[Japanese missions to Tang China|Japanese embassy to Tang China]] in 630 AD. He was also the ancestor of the Inukami clan, from which [[Inukami District, Shiga]] (where the shrine is located) takes its name. However, in the 914 AD ''[[Engishiki]]'' records, it is listed only as a small shrine. From the [[Heian period]] it was revered as having efficiency for childbirth, vocational aid, fire extinguishing and longevity. During then [[Muromachi period]], as pilgrimages to the [[Ise Grand Shrine]] and the [[Kumano shrine|Kumano Sanzan]] gained in popularity, pilgrims were encouraged to visit Taga Shrine as part of the route. In the [[Sengoku period]], [[Toyotomi Hideyoshi]] had a strong faith in this shrine and frequently prayed for an extension of his mother's life. He donated 10,000 ''[[koku]]'', or the equivalent revenue of one year for a ''[[daimyō]]'' for its reconstruction and upkeep. The shrine was destroyed by a fire in 1615, but was rebuilt immediately by [[Shogun]] [[Tokugawa Hidetada]] and reconstructed in 1633 by [[Tokugawa Iemitsu]]. In 1651, [[Ii Naotaka]] of [[Hikone Domain]] granted it estates of 150 ''[[koku]]'' for its upkeep. However, much of the shrine was destroyed again by fire in 1733, and by a storm in 1791. Each time, it was rebuilt with the assistance of the [[Tokugawa shogunate]] and Hikone Domain. With the establishment of [[State Shinto]] following the [[Meiji restoration]], the shrine was designated a prefectural shrine under the [[Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines]] in 1871. It was promoted to a {{nihongo3|Imperial shrine, 2nd rank|官幣中社|[[Kanpei Chūsha]]}} in 1885 and to a {{nihongo3|Imperial shrine, 1st rank|官幣大社|Kanpei-taisha}} in 1914. Many of the shrine's buildings are Registered Tangible Cultural Properties of Taga Town.{{cite web |title=Registered Cultural Properties |url=https://www.town.taga.lg.jp/akebono/bunkazai/shitei.html |website=Taga Town Cultural Properties Center |publisher=Taga Town}}{{in lang|ja}} ==Festivals== *{{nihongo|'''Korei-taisai'''|古例大祭}}: Most important festival held on April 22. Also known as {{nihongo|'''Taga-matsuri'''|多賀まつり}}. *{{nihongo|'''Otaue-sai'''|御田植祭}}: A festival of planting rice with traditional rites held on June 7. *{{nihongo|'''Mantō-sai'''|万灯祭}}: A lantern festival held on the night of August 3–5. ==Specialty goods== *{{nihongo|'''Otaga-shakushi'''|お多賀杓子}}: A talisman ''shakushi'' (Japanese [[Ladle (spoon)|ladle]]) from the reign of [[Empress Genshō]]. Allegedly the origin of the Japanese word ''[[tadpole|otamajakushi]]''. *{{nihongo|'''Itokiri-mochi'''|糸切餅}}: A ''[[wagashi|Japanese sweet]]'' made of ''[[mochi]]'' sticky rice sold at souvenir shops around the shrine. ''Itokiri-mochi'' was first made to celebrate the [[Mongol invasions of Japan|Japanese victory over the Mongols]].{{Citation needed|date=November 2009}} ==Access== The shrine is about a 20 minute walk from [[Taga Taisha-mae Station]] on the [[Ohmi Railway]] [[Ohmi Railway Taga Line|Taga Line]], or about ten minutes by bus from [[Minami-Hikone Station]] on the [[West Japan Railway Company|JR West]] [[Biwako Line]]. ==See also== *[[Takamiya-juku]], the closest of the [[69 Stations of the Nakasendō]] to the shrine ==References== {{Reflist}} ==External links== {{Commons category|Taga-taisha}} * [http://www.tagataisya.or.jp/ Official website] {{in lang|ja}} {{Shinto shrine}} {{Authority control}} [[Category:Shinto shrines in Shiga Prefecture]] [[Category:Places of Scenic Beauty]] [[Category:Taga, Shiga]] [[Category:Ōmi Province]] [[Category:Kanpei Taisha]] [[Category:Sannomiya]] [[Category:Shikinai Shosha]] [[Category:Pet shrines]] [[Category:Taisha]]

Each time, it was rebuilt with the assistance of the [[Tokugawa shogunate]] and Hikone Domain. With the establishment of [[State Shinto]] following the [[Meiji restoration]], the shrine was designated a prefectural shrine under the [[Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines]] in 1871. It was promoted to a {{nihongo3|Imperial shrine, 2nd rank|官幣中社|[[Kanpei Chūsha]]}} in 1885 and to a {{nihongo3|Imperial shrine, 1st rank|官幣大社|Kanpei-taisha}} in 1914. Many of the shrine's buildings are Registered Tangible Cultural Properties of Taga Town.{{cite web |title=Registered Cultural Properties |url=https://www.town.taga.lg.jp/akebono/bunkazai/shitei.html |website=Taga Town Cultural Properties Center |publisher=Taga Town}}{{in lang|ja}} ==Festivals== *{{nihongo|'''Korei-taisai'''|古例大祭}}: Most important festival held on April 22. Also known as {{nihongo|'''Taga-matsuri'''|多賀まつり}}. *{{nihongo|'''Otaue-sai'''|御田植祭}}: A festival of planting rice with traditional rites held on June 7. *{{nihongo|'''Mantō-sai'''|万灯祭}}: A lantern festival held on the night of August 3–5. ==Specialty goods== *{{nihongo|'''Otaga-shakushi'''|お多賀杓子}}: A talisman ''shakushi'' (Japanese [[Ladle (spoon)|ladle]]) from the reign of [[Empress Genshō]]. Allegedly the origin of the Japanese word ''[[tadpole|otamajakushi]]''. *{{nihongo|'''Itokiri-mochi'''|糸切餅}}: A ''[[wagashi|Japanese sweet]]'' made of ''[[mochi]]'' sticky rice sold at souvenir shops around the shrine. ''Itokiri-mochi'' was first made to celebrate the [[Mongol invasions of Japan|Japanese victory over the Mongols]].{{Citation needed|date=November 2009}} ==Access== The shrine is about a 20 minute walk from [[Taga Taisha-mae Station]] on the [[Ohmi Railway]] [[Ohmi Railway Taga Line|Taga Line]], or about ten minutes by bus from [[Minami-Hikone Station]] on the [[West Japan Railway Company|JR West]] [[Biwako Line]]. ==See also== *[[Takamiya-juku]], the closest of the [[69 Stations of the Nakasendō]] to the shrine ==References== {{Reflist}} ==External links== {{Commons category|Taga-taisha}} * [http://www.tagataisya.or.jp/ Official website] {{in lang|ja}} {{Shinto shrine}} {{Authority control}} [[Category:Shinto shrines in Shiga Prefecture]] [[Category:Places of Scenic Beauty]] [[Category:Taga, Shiga]] [[Category:Ōmi Province]] [[Category:Kanpei Taisha]] [[Category:Shikinai Shosha]] [[Category:Taisha-zukuri]]

It was promoted to a {{nihongo3|Imperial shrine, 2nd rank|官幣中社|[[Kanpei Chūsha]]}} in 1885 and to a {{nihongo3|Imperial shrine, 1st rank|官幣大社|Kanpei-taisha}} in 1914. Many of the shrine's buildings are Registered Tangible Cultural Properties of Taga Town.{{cite web |title=Registered Cultural Properties |url=https://www.town.taga.lg.jp/akebono/bunkazai/shitei.html |website=Taga Town Cultural Properties Center |publisher=Taga Town}}{{in lang|ja}} ==Festivals== *{{nihongo|'''Korei-taisai'''|古例大祭}}: Most important festival held on April 22. Also known as {{nihongo|'''Taga-matsuri'''|多賀まつり}}. *{{nihongo|'''Otaue-sai'''|御田植祭}}: A festival of planting rice with traditional rites held on June 7. *{{nihongo|'''Mantō-sai'''|万灯祭}}: A lantern festival held on the night of August 3–5. ==Specialty goods== *{{nihongo|'''Otaga-shakushi'''|お多賀杓子}}: A talisman ''shakushi'' (Japanese [[Ladle (spoon)|ladle]]) from the reign of [[Empress Genshō]]. Allegedly the origin of the Japanese word ''[[tadpole|otamajakushi]]''. *{{nihongo|'''Itokiri-mochi'''|糸切餅}}: A ''[[wagashi|Japanese sweet]]'' made of ''[[mochi]]'' sticky rice sold at souvenir shops around the shrine. ''Itokiri-mochi'' was first made to celebrate the [[Mongol invasions of Japan|Japanese victory over the Mongols]].{{Citation needed|date=November 2009}} ==Access== The shrine is about a 20 minute walk from [[Taga Taisha-mae Station]] on the [[Ohmi Railway]] [[Ohmi Railway Taga Line|Taga Line]], or about ten minutes by bus from [[Minami-Hikone Station]] on the [[West Japan Railway Company|JR West]] [[Biwako Line]]. ==See also== *[[Takamiya-juku]], the closest of the [[69 Stations of the Nakasendō]] to the shrine ==References== {{Reflist}} ==External links== {{Commons category|Taga-taisha}} * [http://www.tagataisya.or.jp/ Official website] {{in lang|ja}} {{Shinto shrine}} {{Authority control}} [[Category:Shinto shrines in Shiga Prefecture]] [[Category:Places of Scenic Beauty]] [[Category:Taga, Shiga]] [[Category:Ōmi Province]] [[Category:Kanpei Taisha]] [[Category:Shikinai Shosha]] [[Category:Taisha]] [[Category:Nagare-zukuri]] {{Izanagi Faith}}

{{Use dmy dates|date=July 2019}} {{Infobox religious building | name = Taga-taisha
多賀大社 | image = Taga taisha1.JPG | caption = ''[[Honden]]'' of Taga-taisha | map_type = Japan Shiga Prefecture#Japan | coordinates = {{Coord|35|13|27|N|136|17|26|E|region:JP_scale:30000_source:jawiki|display=title,inline}} | map_relief = 1 | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | deity = [[Izanagi]], [[Izanami]] | established = Pre-[[Nara period]] | location = 604 Taga, Inukami-gun, Shiga-ken 522-0341 | website = {{URL|http://www.tagataisya.or.jp/}} | footnotes ={{box|background=white|align=center|wide=yes|border size=3px|border color=green|text align=center|[[Monuments of Japan|National Palace of Scenic Beauty]]}} }} [[File:Taga-taisha Haiden-b.jpg|thumb|280px|Haiden]] {{nihongo|'''Taga-taisha'''|多賀大社}} is a [[Shinto]] [[Jinja (Shinto)|shrine]] located in the town of [[Taga, Shiga|Taga]], [[Inukami District, Shiga|Inukami District]], [[Shiga Prefecture]], [[Japan]]. The shrine is frequently referred to as {{nihongo|O-Taga-san|お多賀さん}} by local residents.{{cite web |title=Taga Taisha official home page |url=http://www.tagataisya.or.jp/about/ |website=About Us |publisher=Taga Taisha}}{{in lang|ja}} The gardens of the inner ''[[shoin]]'', which date to the [[Momoyama period]] are a [[Cultural Properties of Japan|nationally designated]] [[Monuments of Japan|Place of Scenic Beauty]],{{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/401/1575 |archive-url=https://archive.today/20120803171529/https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/401/1575 |url-status=dead |archive-date=3 August 2012 |title=多賀神社奥書院庭園 |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |access-date=10 February 2012 }}{{Cite web|title=THE 10 BEST Things to Do Near Taga Taisha, Taga-cho - Tripadvisor|url=https://www.tripadvisor.com/AttractionsNear-g1121270-d1992498-Taga_Taisha-Taga_cho_Inukami_gun_Shiga_Prefecture_Kinki.html|access-date=2021-06-05|website=www.tripadvisor.com|language=en}} whereas the mid-[[Edo period]] ''Shoin'' itself is a Shiga Prefectural Important Cultural Property. ==Main ''kami''== * [[Izanagi]] * [[Izanami]] [[Category:Beppyo shrines]] ==History== The foundation of Taga Taisha is unknown. It is mentioned in the ancient {{Lang|ja-latn|[[Kojiki]]}} and {{Lang|ja-latn|[[Nihon Shoki]]}} chronicles from 712 AD. The shrine's legend connects it with the legendary hero [[Yamato Takeru]], whose son, [[Inugami no Mitasuki]] was sent as an emissary to [[Japanese missions to Sui China|Sui China]] in 614 AD and the first [[Japanese missions to Tang China|Japanese embassy to Tang China]] in 630 AD. He was also the ancestor of the Inukami clan, from which [[Inukami District, Shiga]] (where the shrine is located) takes its name. However, in the 914 AD ''[[Engishiki]]'' records, it is listed only as a small shrine. From the [[Heian period]] it was revered as having efficiency for childbirth, vocational aid, fire extinguishing and longevity. During then [[Muromachi period]], as pilgrimages to the [[Ise Grand Shrine]] and the [[Kumano shrine|Kumano Sanzan]] gained in popularity, pilgrims were encouraged to visit Taga Shrine as part of the route. In the [[Sengoku period]], [[Toyotomi Hideyoshi]] had a strong faith in this shrine and frequently prayed for an extension of his mother's life. He donated 10,000 ''[[koku]]'', or the equivalent revenue of one year for a ''[[daimyō]]'' for its reconstruction and upkeep. The shrine was destroyed by a fire in 1615, but was rebuilt immediately by [[Shogun]] [[Tokugawa Hidetada]] and reconstructed in 1633 by [[Tokugawa Iemitsu]]. In 1651, [[Ii Naotaka]] of [[Hikone Domain]] granted it estates of 150 ''[[koku]]'' for its upkeep. However, much of the shrine was destroyed again by fire in 1733, and by a storm in 1791. Each time, it was rebuilt with the assistance of the [[Tokugawa shogunate]] and Hikone Domain. With the establishment of [[State Shinto]] following the [[Meiji restoration]], the shrine was designated a prefectural shrine under the [[Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines]] in 1871. It was promoted to a {{nihongo3|Imperial shrine, 2nd rank|官幣中社|[[Kanpei Chūsha]]}} in 1885 and to a {{nihongo3|Imperial shrine, 1st rank|官幣大社|Kanpei-taisha}} in 1914. Many of the shrine's buildings are Registered Tangible Cultural Properties of Taga Town.{{cite web |title=Registered Cultural Properties |url=https://www.town.taga.lg.jp/akebono/bunkazai/shitei.html |website=Taga Town Cultural Properties Center |publisher=Taga Town}}{{in lang|ja}} ==Festivals== *{{nihongo|'''Korei-taisai'''|古例大祭}}: Most important festival held on April 22. Also known as {{nihongo|'''Taga-matsuri'''|多賀まつり}}. *{{nihongo|'''Otaue-sai'''|御田植祭}}: A festival of planting rice with traditional rites held on June 7. *{{nihongo|'''Mantō-sai'''|万灯祭}}: A lantern festival held on the night of August 3–5. ==Specialty goods== *{{nihongo|'''Otaga-shakushi'''|お多賀杓子}}: A talisman ''shakushi'' (Japanese [[Ladle (spoon)|ladle]]) from the reign of [[Empress Genshō]]. Allegedly the origin of the Japanese word ''[[tadpole|otamajakushi]]''. *{{nihongo|'''Itokiri-mochi'''|糸切餅}}: A ''[[wagashi|Japanese sweet]]'' made of ''[[mochi]]'' sticky rice sold at souvenir shops around the shrine. ''Itokiri-mochi'' was first made to celebrate the [[Mongol invasions of Japan|Japanese victory over the Mongols]].{{Citation needed|date=November 2009}} ==Access== The shrine is about a 20 minute walk from [[Taga Taisha-mae Station]] on the [[Ohmi Railway]] [[Ohmi Railway Taga Line|Taga Line]], or about ten minutes by bus from [[Minami-Hikone Station]] on the [[West Japan Railway Company|JR West]] [[Biwako Line]]. ==See also== *[[Takamiya-juku]], the closest of the [[69 Stations of the Nakasendō]] to the shrine ==References== {{Reflist}} ==External links== {{Commons category|Taga-taisha}} * [http://www.tagataisya.or.jp/ Official website] {{in lang|ja}} {{Shinto shrine}} {{Authority control}} [[Category:Shinto shrines in Shiga Prefecture]] [[Category:Places of Scenic Beauty]] [[Category:Taga, Shiga]] [[Category:Ōmi Province]] [[Category:Kanpei Taisha]] [[Category:Uncategorized_Shinto_shrines]][[en:Taga-taisha]][[Category:Uncategorized_Shinto_shrines]] [[Category:Shikinai Shosha]] [[Category:Taisha]] [[Category:Nagare-zukuri]] {{Izanagi Faith}}

During then [[Muromachi period]], as pilgrimages to the [[Ise Grand Shrine]] and the [[Kumano shrine|Kumano Sanzan]] gained in popularity, pilgrims were encouraged to visit Taga Shrine as part of the route. In the [[Sengoku period]], [[Toyotomi Hideyoshi]] had a strong faith in this shrine and frequently prayed for an extension of his mother's life. He donated 10,000 ''[[koku]]'', or the equivalent revenue of one year for a ''[[daimyō]]'' for its reconstruction and upkeep. The shrine was destroyed by a fire in 1615, but was rebuilt immediately by [[Shogun]] [[Tokugawa Hidetada]] and reconstructed in 1633 by [[Tokugawa Iemitsu]]. In 1651, [[Ii Naotaka]] of [[Hikone Domain]] granted it estates of 150 ''[[koku]]'' for its upkeep. However, much of the shrine was destroyed again by fire in 1733, and by a storm in 1791. Each time, it was rebuilt with the assistance of the [[Tokugawa shogunate]] and Hikone Domain. With the establishment of [[State Shinto]] following the [[Meiji restoration]], the shrine was designated a prefectural shrine under the [[Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines]] in 1871. It was promoted to a {{nihongo3|Imperial shrine, 2nd rank|官幣中社|[[Kanpei Chūsha]]}} in 1885 and to a {{nihongo3|Imperial shrine, 1st rank|官幣大社|Kanpei-taisha}} in 1914. Many of the shrine's buildings are Registered Tangible Cultural Properties of Taga Town.{{cite web |title=Registered Cultural Properties |url=https://www.town.taga.lg.jp/akebono/bunkazai/shitei.html |website=Taga Town Cultural Properties Center |publisher=Taga Town}}{{in lang|ja}} ==Festivals== *{{nihongo|'''Korei-taisai'''|古例大祭}}: Most important festival held on April 22. Also known as {{nihongo|'''Taga-matsuri'''|多賀まつり}}. *{{nihongo|'''Otaue-sai'''|御田植祭}}: A festival of planting rice with traditional rites held on June 7. *{{nihongo|'''Mantō-sai'''|万灯祭}}: A lantern festival held on the night of August 3–5. ==Specialty goods== *{{nihongo|'''Otaga-shakushi'''|お多賀杓子}}: A talisman ''shakushi'' (Japanese [[Ladle (spoon)|ladle]]) from the reign of [[Empress Genshō]]. Allegedly the origin of the Japanese word ''[[tadpole|otamajakushi]]''. *{{nihongo|'''Itokiri-mochi'''|糸切餅}}: A ''[[wagashi|Japanese sweet]]'' made of ''[[mochi]]'' sticky rice sold at souvenir shops around the shrine. ''Itokiri-mochi'' was first made to celebrate the [[Mongol invasions of Japan|Japanese victory over the Mongols]].{{Citation needed|date=November 2009}} ==Access== The shrine is about a 20 minute walk from [[Taga Taisha-mae Station]] on the [[Ohmi Railway]] [[Ohmi Railway Taga Line|Taga Line]], or about ten minutes by bus from [[Minami-Hikone Station]] on the [[West Japan Railway Company|JR West]] [[Biwako Line]]. ==See also== *[[Takamiya-juku]], the closest of the [[69 Stations of the Nakasendō]] to the shrine ==References== {{Reflist}} ==External links== {{Commons category|Taga-taisha}} * [http://www.tagataisya.or.jp/ Official website] {{in lang|ja}} {{Shinto shrine}} {{Authority control}} [[Category:Shinto shrines in Shiga Prefecture]] [[Category:Places of Scenic Beauty]] [[Category:Taga, Shiga]] [[Category:Ōmi Province]] [[Category:Kanpei Taisha]] [[en:Taga-taisha]][[Category:Shikinai Shosha]] [[Category:Taisha]] [[Category:Nagare-zukuri]] [[Category:Pages from wikipedia]] [[Category:Pages from wikipedia]][[en:Taga-taisha]][[category:dd]] {{Izanagi Faith}}

多賀大社 | image = Taga taisha1.JPG | caption = ''[[Honden]]'' of Taga-taisha | map_type = Japan Shiga Prefecture#Japan | coordinates = {{Coord|35|13|27|N|136|17|26|E|region:JP_scale:30000_source:jawiki|display=title,inline}} | map_relief = 1 | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | deity = [[Izanagi]], [[Izanami]] | established = Pre-[[Nara period]] | location = 604 Taga, Inukami-gun, Shiga-ken 522-0341 | website = {{URL|http://www.tagataisya.or.jp/}} | footnotes ={{box|background=white|align=center|wide=yes|border size=3px|border color=green|text align=center|[[Monuments of Japan|National Palace of Scenic Beauty]]}} }} [[File:Taga-taisha Haiden-b.jpg|thumb|280px|Haiden]] {{nihongo|'''Taga-taisha'''|多賀大社}} is a [[Shinto]] [[Jinja (Shinto)|shrine]] located in the town of [[Taga, Shiga|Taga]], [[Inukami District, Shiga|Inukami District]], [[Shiga Prefecture]], [[Japan]]. The shrine is frequently referred to as {{nihongo|O-Taga-san|お多賀さん}} by local residents.{{cite web |title=Taga Taisha official home page |url=http://www.tagataisya.or.jp/about/ |website=About Us |publisher=Taga Taisha}}{{in lang|ja}} The gardens of the inner ''[[shoin]]'', which date to the [[Momoyama period]] are a [[Cultural Properties of Japan|nationally designated]] [[Monuments of Japan|Place of Scenic Beauty]],{{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/401/1575 |archive-url=https://archive.today/20120803171529/https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/401/1575 |url-status=dead |archive-date=3 August 2012 |title=多賀神社奥書院庭園 |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |access-date=10 February 2012 }}{{Cite web|title=THE 10 BEST Things to Do Near Taga Taisha, Taga-cho - Tripadvisor|url=https://www.tripadvisor.com/AttractionsNear-g1121270-d1992498-Taga_Taisha-Taga_cho_Inukami_gun_Shiga_Prefecture_Kinki.html|access-date=2021-06-05|website=www.tripadvisor.com|language=en}} whereas the mid-[[Edo period]] ''Shoin'' itself is a Shiga Prefectural Important Cultural Property. ==Main ''kami''== * [[Izanagi]] * [[Izanami]] [[Category:Beppyo shrines]] ==History== The foundation of Taga Taisha is unknown. It is mentioned in the ancient {{Lang|ja-latn|[[Kojiki]]}} and {{Lang|ja-latn|[[Nihon Shoki]]}} chronicles from 712 AD. The shrine's legend connects it with the legendary hero [[Yamato Takeru]], whose son, [[Inugami no Mitasuki]] was sent as an emissary to [[Japanese missions to Sui China|Sui China]] in 614 AD and the first [[Japanese missions to Tang China|Japanese embassy to Tang China]] in 630 AD. He was also the ancestor of the Inukami clan, from which [[Inukami District, Shiga]] (where the shrine is located) takes its name. However, in the 914 AD ''[[Engishiki]]'' records, it is listed only as a small shrine. From the [[Heian period]] it was revered as having efficiency for childbirth, vocational aid, fire extinguishing and longevity. During then [[Muromachi period]], as pilgrimages to the [[Ise Grand Shrine]] and the [[Kumano shrine|Kumano Sanzan]] gained in popularity, pilgrims were encouraged to visit Taga Shrine as part of the route. In the [[Sengoku period]], [[Toyotomi Hideyoshi]] had a strong faith in this shrine and frequently prayed for an extension of his mother's life. He donated 10,000 ''[[koku]]'', or the equivalent revenue of one year for a ''[[daimyō]]'' for its reconstruction and upkeep. The shrine was destroyed by a fire in 1615, but was rebuilt immediately by [[Shogun]] [[Tokugawa Hidetada]] and reconstructed in 1633 by [[Tokugawa Iemitsu]]. In 1651, [[Ii Naotaka]] of [[Hikone Domain]] granted it estates of 150 ''[[koku]]'' for its upkeep. However, much of the shrine was destroyed again by fire in 1733, and by a storm in 1791. Each time, it was rebuilt with the assistance of the [[Tokugawa shogunate]] and Hikone Domain. With the establishment of [[State Shinto]] following the [[Meiji restoration]], the shrine was designated a prefectural shrine under the [[Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines]] in 1871. It was promoted to a {{nihongo3|Imperial shrine, 2nd rank|官幣中社|[[Kanpei Chūsha]]}} in 1885 and to a {{nihongo3|Imperial shrine, 1st rank|官幣大社|Kanpei-taisha}} in 1914. Many of the shrine's buildings are Registered Tangible Cultural Properties of Taga Town.{{cite web |title=Registered Cultural Properties |url=https://www.town.taga.lg.jp/akebono/bunkazai/shitei.html |website=Taga Town Cultural Properties Center |publisher=Taga Town}}{{in lang|ja}} ==Festivals== *{{nihongo|'''Korei-taisai'''|古例大祭}}: Most important festival held on April 22. Allegedly the origin of the Japanese word ''[[tadpole|otamajakushi]]''. *{{nihongo|'''Itokiri-mochi'''|糸切餅}}: A ''[[wagashi|Japanese sweet]]'' made of ''[[mochi]]'' sticky rice sold at souvenir shops around the shrine. ''Itokiri-mochi'' was first made to celebrate the [[Mongol invasions of Japan|Japanese victory over the Mongols]].{{Citation needed|date=November 2009}} ==Access== The shrine is about a 20 minute walk from [[Taga Taisha-mae Station]] on the [[Ohmi Railway]] [[Ohmi Railway Taga Line|Taga Line]], or about ten minutes by bus from [[Minami-Hikone Station]] on the [[West Japan Railway Company|JR West]] [[Biwako Line]]. ==See also== *[[Takamiya-juku]], the closest of the [[69 Stations of the Nakasendō]] to the shrine ==References== {{Reflist}} ==External links== {{Commons category|Taga-taisha}} * [http://www.tagataisya.or.jp/ Official website] {{in lang|ja}} {{Shinto shrine}} {{Authority control}} [[Category:Shinto shrines in Shiga Prefecture]] [[Category:Places of Scenic Beauty]] [[Category:Taga, Shiga]] [[Category:Ōmi Province]] [[Category:Kanpei Taisha]] [[en:Taga-taisha]][[Category:Shikinai Shosha]] [[Category:Taisha]] [[Category:Nagare-zukuri]] [[Category:Pages from wikipedia]] [[Category:Pages from wikipedia]][[en:Taga-taisha]][[category:dd]] [[Category:Pages from wikipedia]][[en:Taga-taisha]][[category:dd]] {{Izanagi Faith}}
