It was promoted to a {{nihongo3|Imperial shrine, 2nd rank|官幣中社|[[Kanpei Chūsha]]}} in 1885 and to a {{nihongo3|Imperial shrine, 1st rank|官幣大社|Kanpei-taisha}} in 1914. Many of the shrine's buildings are Registered Tangible Cultural Properties of Taga Town.{{cite web |title=Registered Cultural Properties |url=https://www.town.taga.lg.jp/akebono/bunkazai/shitei.html |website=Taga Town Cultural Properties Center |publisher=Taga Town}}{{in lang|ja}} ==Festivals== *{{nihongo|'''Korei-taisai'''|古例大祭}}: Most important festival held on April 22. Also known as {{nihongo|'''Taga-matsuri'''|多賀まつり}}. *{{nihongo|'''Otaue-sai'''|御田植祭}}: A festival of planting rice with traditional rites held on June 7. *{{nihongo|'''Mantō-sai'''|万灯祭}}: A lantern festival held on the night of August 3–5. ==Specialty goods== *{{nihongo|'''Otaga-shakushi'''|お多賀杓子}}: A talisman ''shakushi'' (Japanese [[Ladle (spoon)|ladle]]) from the reign of [[Empress Genshō]]. Allegedly the origin of the Japanese word ''[[tadpole|otamajakushi]]''. *{{nihongo|'''Itokiri-mochi'''|糸切餅}}: A ''[[wagashi|Japanese sweet]]'' made of ''[[mochi]]'' sticky rice sold at souvenir shops around the shrine. ''Itokiri-mochi'' was first made to celebrate the [[Mongol invasions of Japan|Japanese victory over the Mongols]].{{Citation needed|date=November 2009}} ==Access== The shrine is about a 20 minute walk from [[Taga Taisha-mae Station]] on the [[Ohmi Railway]] [[Ohmi Railway Taga Line|Taga Line]], or about ten minutes by bus from [[Minami-Hikone Station]] on the [[West Japan Railway Company|JR West]] [[Biwako Line]]. ==See also== *[[Takamiya-juku]], the closest of the [[69 Stations of the Nakasendō]] to the shrine ==References== {{Reflist}} ==External links== {{Commons Category|Taga-taisha}} * [http://www.tagataisya.or.jp/ Official website] {{in lang|ja}} {{Shinto shrine}} [[Category:Pages that used to use Authority control]][[en:Taga-taisha]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Shiga Prefecture]] [[Category:Places of Scenic Beauty]] [[Category:Taga, Shiga]] [[Category:Ōmi Province]] [[Category:Kanpei Taisha]] [[en:Taga-taisha]][[Category:Shikinai Shosha]] [[Category:Taisha]] [[Category:Nagare-zukuri]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia 1 6]][[en:Taga-taisha]]]] [[Category:mm]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia 1 6]][[en:Taga-taisha]]]] [[Category:mm]] [[en:Taga-taisha]][[Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia 1 6]][[en:Taga-taisha]]]] [[Category:mm]] [[Category:dd]][[Category:Taga-taisha]][[Category:dd]] [[Category:Bump-01-21]] [[Category:Bump-01-21]] [[Category:bump 1-26]] [[Category:second attempt at refreshing on January 27 2024]][[Category:Taga-taisha]][[Category:second attempt at refreshing on January 27 2024]] [[Category:kk]][[en:Taga-taisha]][[Category:kk]] [[Category:kk]][[en:Taga-taisha]][[Category:kk]] [[Category:qq]][[en:Taga-taisha]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:qq]][[en:Taga-taisha]][[Category:qq]] [[en:Taga-taisha]]{{Izanagi Faith}}

{{Use dmy dates|date=July 2019}} {{Infobox religious building | name = Taga-taisha
多賀大社 | image = Taga taisha1.JPG | caption = ''[[Honden]]'' of Taga-taisha | map_type = Japan Shiga Prefecture#Japan | coordinates = {{Coord|35|13|27|N|136|17|26|E|region:JP_scale:30000_source:jawiki|display=title,inline}} | map_relief = 1 | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | deity = [[Izanagi]], [[Izanami]] | established = Pre-[[Nara period]] | location = 604 Taga, Inukami-gun, Shiga-ken 522-0341 | website = {{URL|http://www.tagataisya.or.jp/}} | footnotes ={{box|background=white|align=center|wide=yes|border size=3px|border color=green|text align=center|[[Monuments of Japan|National Palace of Scenic Beauty]]}} }} [[File:Taga-taisha Haiden-b.jpg|thumb|280px|Haiden]] {{nihongo|'''Taga-taisha'''|多賀大社}} is a [[Shinto]] [[Jinja (Shinto)|shrine]] located in the town of [[Taga, Shiga|Taga]], [[Inukami District, Shiga|Inukami District]], [[Shiga Prefecture]], [[Japan]]. The shrine is frequently referred to as {{nihongo|O-Taga-san|お多賀さん}} by local residents.{{cite web |title=Taga Taisha official home page |url=http://www.tagataisya.or.jp/about/ |website=About Us |publisher=Taga Taisha}}{{in lang|ja}} The gardens of the inner ''[[shoin]]'', which date to the [[Momoyama period]] are a [[Cultural Properties of Japan|nationally designated]] [[Monuments of Japan|Place of Scenic Beauty]],{{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/401/1575 |archive-url=https://archive.today/20120803171529/https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/401/1575 |url-status=dead |archive-date=3 August 2012 |title=多賀神社奥書院庭園 |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |access-date=10 February 2012 }}{{Cite web|title=THE 10 BEST Things to Do Near Taga Taisha, Taga-cho - Tripadvisor|url=https://www.tripadvisor.com/AttractionsNear-g1121270-d1992498-Taga_Taisha-Taga_cho_Inukami_gun_Shiga_Prefecture_Kinki.html|access-date=2021-06-05|website=www.tripadvisor.com|language=en}} whereas the mid-[[Edo period]] ''Shoin'' itself is a Shiga Prefectural Important Cultural Property. ==Main ''kami''== * [[Izanagi]] * [[Izanami]] [[Category:Beppyo shrines]] [[Category:beppyo shrines without templates]] ==History== The foundation of Taga Taisha is unknown. It is mentioned in the ancient {{Lang|ja-latn|[[Kojiki]]}} and {{Lang|ja-latn|[[Nihon Shoki]]}} chronicles from 712 AD. The shrine's legend connects it with the legendary hero [[Yamato Takeru]], whose son, [[Inugami no Mitasuki]] was sent as an emissary to [[Japanese missions to Sui China|Sui China]] in 614 AD and the first [[Japanese missions to Tang China|Japanese embassy to Tang China]] in 630 AD. He was also the ancestor of the Inukami clan, from which [[Inukami District, Shiga]] (where the shrine is located) takes its name. However, in the 914 AD ''[[Engishiki]]'' records, it is listed only as a small shrine. From the [[Heian period]] it was revered as having efficiency for childbirth, vocational aid, fire extinguishing and longevity. During then [[Muromachi period]], as pilgrimages to the [[Ise Grand Shrine]] and the [[Kumano shrine|Kumano Sanzan]] gained in popularity, pilgrims were encouraged to visit Taga Shrine as part of the route. In the [[Sengoku period]], [[Toyotomi Hideyoshi]] had a strong faith in this shrine and frequently prayed for an extension of his mother's life. He donated 10,000 ''[[koku]]'', or the equivalent revenue of one year for a ''[[daimyō]]'' for its reconstruction and upkeep. The shrine was destroyed by a fire in 1615, but was rebuilt immediately by [[Shogun]] [[Tokugawa Hidetada]] and reconstructed in 1633 by [[Tokugawa Iemitsu]]. In 1651, [[Ii Naotaka]] of [[Hikone Domain]] granted it estates of 150 ''[[koku]]'' for its upkeep. However, much of the shrine was destroyed again by fire in 1733, and by a storm in 1791. Each time, it was rebuilt with the assistance of the [[Tokugawa shogunate]] and Hikone Domain. With the establishment of [[State Shinto]] following the [[Meiji restoration]], the shrine was designated a prefectural shrine under the [[Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines]] in 1871. It was promoted to a {{nihongo3|Imperial shrine, 2nd rank|官幣中社|[[Kanpei Chūsha]]}} in 1885 and to a {{nihongo3|Imperial shrine, 1st rank|官幣大社|Kanpei-taisha}} in 1914. Many of the shrine's buildings are Registered Tangible Cultural Properties of Taga Town.{{cite web |title=Registered Cultural Properties |url=https://www.town.taga.lg.jp/akebono/bunkazai/shitei.html |website=Taga Town Cultural Properties Center |publisher=Taga Town}}{{in lang|ja}} ==Festivals== *{{nihongo|'''Korei-taisai'''|古例大祭}}: Most important festival held on April 22. Also known as {{nihongo|'''Taga-matsuri'''|多賀まつり}}. *{{nihongo|'''Otaue-sai'''|御田植祭}}: A festival of planting rice with traditional rites held on June 7. *{{nihongo|'''Mantō-sai'''|万灯祭}}: A lantern festival held on the night of August 3–5. ==Specialty goods== *{{nihongo|'''Otaga-shakushi'''|お多賀杓子}}: A talisman ''shakushi'' (Japanese [[Ladle (spoon)|ladle]]) from the reign of [[Empress Genshō]]. Allegedly the origin of the Japanese word ''[[tadpole|otamajakushi]]''. *{{nihongo|'''Itokiri-mochi'''|糸切餅}}: A ''[[wagashi|Japanese sweet]]'' made of ''[[mochi]]'' sticky rice sold at souvenir shops around the shrine. ''Itokiri-mochi'' was first made to celebrate the [[Mongol invasions of Japan|Japanese victory over the Mongols]].{{Citation needed|date=November 2009}} ==Access== The shrine is about a 20 minute walk from [[Taga Taisha-mae Station]] on the [[Ohmi Railway]] [[Ohmi Railway Taga Line|Taga Line]], or about ten minutes by bus from [[Minami-Hikone Station]] on the [[West Japan Railway Company|JR West]] [[Biwako Line]]. ==See also== *[[Takamiya-juku]], the closest of the [[69 Stations of the Nakasendō]] to the shrine ==References== {{Reflist}} ==External links== {{Commons Category|Taga-taisha}} * [http://www.tagataisya.or.jp/ Official website] {{in lang|ja}} {{Shinto shrine}} [[Category:Pages that used to use Authority control]][[en:Taga-taisha]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Shiga Prefecture]] [[Category:Places of Scenic Beauty]] [[Category:Taga, Shiga]] [[Category:Ōmi Province]] [[Category:Kanpei Taisha]] [[en:Taga-taisha]][[Category:Shikinai Shosha]] [[Category:Taisha]] [[Category:Nagare-zukuri]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia 1 6]][[en:Taga-taisha]]]] [[Category:mm]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia 1 6]][[en:Taga-taisha]]]] [[Category:mm]] [[en:Taga-taisha]][[Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia 1 6]][[en:Taga-taisha]]]] [[Category:mm]] [[Category:dd]][[Category:Taga-taisha]][[Category:dd]] [[Category:Bump-01-21]] [[Category:Bump-01-21]] [[Category:bump 1-26]] [[Category:second attempt at refreshing on January 27 2024]][[Category:Taga-taisha]][[Category:second attempt at refreshing on January 27 2024]] [[Category:kk]][[en:Taga-taisha]][[Category:kk]] [[Category:kk]][[en:Taga-taisha]][[Category:kk]] [[Category:qq]][[en:Taga-taisha]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:qq]][[en:Taga-taisha]][[Category:qq]] [[en:Taga-taisha]]{{Izanagi Faith}}

''Itokiri-mochi'' was first made to celebrate the [[Mongol invasions of Japan|Japanese victory over the Mongols]].{{Citation needed|date=November 2009}} ==Access== The shrine is about a 20 minute walk from [[Taga Taisha-mae Station]] on the [[Ohmi Railway]] [[Ohmi Railway Taga Line|Taga Line]], or about ten minutes by bus from [[Minami-Hikone Station]] on the [[West Japan Railway Company|JR West]] [[Biwako Line]]. ==See also== *[[Takamiya-juku]], the closest of the [[69 Stations of the Nakasendō]] to the shrine ==References== {{Reflist}} ==External links== {{Commons Category|Taga-taisha}} * [http://www.tagataisya.or.jp/ Official website] {{in lang|ja}} {{Shinto shrine}} [[Category:Pages that used to use Authority control]][[en:Taga-taisha]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Shiga Prefecture]] [[Category:Places of Scenic Beauty]] [[Category:Taga, Shiga]] [[Category:Ōmi Province]] [[Category:Kanpei Taisha]] [[en:Taga-taisha]][[Category:Shikinai Shosha]] [[Category:Taisha]] [[Category:Nagare-zukuri]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia 1 6]][[en:Taga-taisha]]]] [[Category:mm]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia 1 6]][[en:Taga-taisha]]]] [[Category:mm]] [[en:Taga-taisha]][[Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia 1 6]][[en:Taga-taisha]]]] [[Category:mm]] [[Category:dd]][[Category:Taga-taisha]][[Category:dd]] [[Category:Bump-01-21]] [[Category:Bump-01-21]] [[Category:bump 1-26]] [[Category:second attempt at refreshing on January 27 2024]][[Category:Taga-taisha]][[Category:second attempt at refreshing on January 27 2024]] [[Category:kk]][[en:Taga-taisha]][[Category:kk]] [[Category:kk]][[en:Taga-taisha]][[Category:kk]] [[Category:qq]][[en:Taga-taisha]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:qq]][[en:Taga-taisha]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Taga-taisha]][[Category:qq]] [[en:Taga-taisha]]{{Izanagi Faith}}

{{Use dmy dates|date=July 2019}} {{Infobox religious building | name = Taga-taisha
多賀大社 | image = Taga taisha1.JPG | caption = ''[[Honden]]'' of Taga-taisha | map_type = Japan Shiga Prefecture#Japan | coordinates = {{Coord|35|13|27|N|136|17|26|E|region:JP_scale:30000_source:jawiki|display=title,inline}} | map_relief = 1 | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | deity = [[Izanagi]], [[Izanami]] | established = Pre-[[Nara period]] | location = 604 Taga, Inukami-gun, Shiga-ken 522-0341 | website = {{URL|http://www.tagataisya.or.jp/}} | footnotes ={{box|background=white|align=center|wide=yes|border size=3px|border color=green|text align=center|[[Monuments of Japan|National Palace of Scenic Beauty]]}} }} [[File:Taga-taisha Haiden-b.jpg|thumb|280px|Haiden]] {{nihongo|'''Taga-taisha'''|多賀大社}} is a [[Shinto]] [[Jinja (Shinto)|shrine]] located in the town of [[Taga, Shiga|Taga]], [[Inukami District, Shiga|Inukami District]], [[Shiga Prefecture]], [[Japan]]. The shrine is frequently referred to as {{nihongo|O-Taga-san|お多賀さん}} by local residents.{{cite web |title=Taga Taisha official home page |url=http://www.tagataisya.or.jp/about/ |website=About Us |publisher=Taga Taisha}}{{in lang|ja}} The gardens of the inner ''[[shoin]]'', which date to the [[Momoyama period]] are a [[Cultural Properties of Japan|nationally designated]] [[Monuments of Japan|Place of Scenic Beauty]],{{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/401/1575 |archive-url=https://archive.today/20120803171529/https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/401/1575 |url-status=dead |archive-date=3 August 2012 |title=多賀神社奥書院庭園 |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |access-date=10 February 2012 }}{{Cite web|title=THE 10 BEST Things to Do Near Taga Taisha, Taga-cho - Tripadvisor|url=https://www.tripadvisor.com/AttractionsNear-g1121270-d1992498-Taga_Taisha-Taga_cho_Inukami_gun_Shiga_Prefecture_Kinki.html|access-date=2021-06-05|website=www.tripadvisor.com|language=en}} whereas the mid-[[Edo period]] ''Shoin'' itself is a Shiga Prefectural Important Cultural Property. ==Main ''kami''== * [[Izanagi]] * [[Izanami]] [[Category:Beppyo shrines]] [[Category:bgoodbye $1]] ==History== The foundation of Taga Taisha is unknown. It is mentioned in the ancient {{Lang|ja-latn|[[Kojiki]]}} and {{Lang|ja-latn|[[Nihon Shoki]]}} chronicles from 712 AD. The shrine's legend connects it with the legendary hero [[Yamato Takeru]], whose son, [[Inugami no Mitasuki]] was sent as an emissary to [[Japanese missions to Sui China|Sui China]] in 614 AD and the first [[Japanese missions to Tang China|Japanese embassy to Tang China]] in 630 AD. He was also the ancestor of the Inukami clan, from which [[Inukami District, Shiga]] (where the shrine is located) takes its name. However, in the 914 AD ''[[Engishiki]]'' records, it is listed only as a small shrine. From the [[Heian period]] it was revered as having efficiency for childbirth, vocational aid, fire extinguishing and longevity. During then [[Muromachi period]], as pilgrimages to the [[Ise Grand Shrine]] and the [[Kumano shrine|Kumano Sanzan]] gained in popularity, pilgrims were encouraged to visit Taga Shrine as part of the route. In the [[Sengoku period]], [[Toyotomi Hideyoshi]] had a strong faith in this shrine and frequently prayed for an extension of his mother's life. He donated 10,000 ''[[koku]]'', or the equivalent revenue of one year for a ''[[daimyō]]'' for its reconstruction and upkeep. The shrine was destroyed by a fire in 1615, but was rebuilt immediately by [[Shogun]] [[Tokugawa Hidetada]] and reconstructed in 1633 by [[Tokugawa Iemitsu]]. In 1651, [[Ii Naotaka]] of [[Hikone Domain]] granted it estates of 150 ''[[koku]]'' for its upkeep. However, much of the shrine was destroyed again by fire in 1733, and by a storm in 1791. Each time, it was rebuilt with the assistance of the [[Tokugawa shogunate]] and Hikone Domain. With the establishment of [[State Shinto]] following the [[Meiji restoration]], the shrine was designated a prefectural shrine under the [[Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines]] in 1871. It was promoted to a {{nihongo3|Imperial shrine, 2nd rank|官幣中社|[[Kanpei Chūsha]]}} in 1885 and to a {{nihongo3|Imperial shrine, 1st rank|官幣大社|Kanpei-taisha}} in 1914. Many of the shrine's buildings are Registered Tangible Cultural Properties of Taga Town.{{cite web |title=Registered Cultural Properties |url=https://www.town.taga.lg.jp/akebono/bunkazai/shitei.html |website=Taga Town Cultural Properties Center |publisher=Taga Town}}{{in lang|ja}} ==Festivals== *{{nihongo|'''Korei-taisai'''|古例大祭}}: Most important festival held on April 22. Also known as {{nihongo|'''Taga-matsuri'''|多賀まつり}}. *{{nihongo|'''Otaue-sai'''|御田植祭}}: A festival of planting rice with traditional rites held on June 7. *{{nihongo|'''Mantō-sai'''|万灯祭}}: A lantern festival held on the night of August 3–5. ==Specialty goods== *{{nihongo|'''Otaga-shakushi'''|お多賀杓子}}: A talisman ''shakushi'' (Japanese [[Ladle (spoon)|ladle]]) from the reign of [[Empress Genshō]]. Allegedly the origin of the Japanese word ''[[tadpole|otamajakushi]]''. *{{nihongo|'''Itokiri-mochi'''|糸切餅}}: A ''[[wagashi|Japanese sweet]]'' made of ''[[mochi]]'' sticky rice sold at souvenir shops around the shrine. ''Itokiri-mochi'' was first made to celebrate the [[Mongol invasions of Japan|Japanese victory over the Mongols]].{{Citation needed|date=November 2009}} ==Access== The shrine is about a 20 minute walk from [[Taga Taisha-mae Station]] on the [[Ohmi Railway]] [[Ohmi Railway Taga Line|Taga Line]], or about ten minutes by bus from [[Minami-Hikone Station]] on the [[West Japan Railway Company|JR West]] [[Biwako Line]]. ==See also== *[[Takamiya-juku]], the closest of the [[69 Stations of the Nakasendō]] to the shrine ==References== {{Reflist}} ==External links== {{Commons Category|Taga-taisha}} * [http://www.tagataisya.or.jp/ Official website] {{in lang|ja}} {{Shinto shrine}} [[Category:Pages that used to use Authority control]][[en:Taga-taisha]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Shiga Prefecture]] [[Category:Places of Scenic Beauty]] [[Category:Taga, Shiga]] [[Category:Ōmi Province]] [[Category:Kanpei Taisha]] [[en:Taga-taisha]][[Category:Shikinai Shosha]] [[Category:Taisha]] [[Category:Nagare-zukuri]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia 1 6]][[en:Taga-taisha]]]] [[Category:mm]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia 1 6]][[en:Taga-taisha]]]] [[Category:mm]] [[en:Taga-taisha]][[Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia 1 6]][[en:Taga-taisha]]]] [[Category:mm]] [[Category:dd]][[Category:Taga-taisha]][[Category:dd]] [[Category:Bump-01-21]] [[Category:Bump-01-21]] [[Category:bump 1-26]] [[Category:second attempt at refreshing on January 27 2024]][[Category:Taga-taisha]][[Category:second attempt at refreshing on January 27 2024]] [[Category:kk]][[en:Taga-taisha]][[Category:kk]] [[Category:kk]][[en:Taga-taisha]][[Category:kk]] [[Category:qq]][[en:Taga-taisha]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:qq]][[en:Taga-taisha]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Taga-taisha]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:qqq]][[en:Taga-taisha]][[Category:qq]] [[en:Taga-taisha]]{{Izanagi Faith}}

In 1651, [[Ii Naotaka]] of [[Hikone Domain]] granted it estates of 150 ''[[koku]]'' for its upkeep. However, much of the shrine was destroyed again by fire in 1733, and by a storm in 1791. Each time, it was rebuilt with the assistance of the [[Tokugawa shogunate]] and Hikone Domain. {{Use dmy dates|date=July 2019}} {{Infobox religious building | name = Taga-taisha
多賀大社 | image = Taga taisha1.JPG | caption = ''[[Honden]]'' of Taga-taisha | map_type = Japan Shiga Prefecture#Japan | coordinates = {{Coord|35|13|27|N|136|17|26|E|region:JP_scale:30000_source:jawiki|display=title,inline}} | map_relief = 1 | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | deity = [[Izanagi]], [[Izanami]] | established = Pre-[[Nara period]] | location = 604 Taga, Inukami-gun, Shiga-ken 522-0341 | website = {{URL|http://www.tagataisya.or.jp/}} | footnotes ={{box|background=white|align=center|wide=yes|border size=3px|border color=green|text align=center|[[Monuments of Japan|National Palace of Scenic Beauty]]}} }} [[File:Taga-taisha Haiden-b.jpg|thumb|280px|Haiden]] {{nihongo|'''Taga-taisha'''|多賀大社}} is a [[Shinto]] [[Jinja (Shinto)|shrine]] located in the town of [[Taga, Shiga|Taga]], [[Inukami District, Shiga|Inukami District]], [[Shiga Prefecture]], [[Japan]]. The shrine is frequently referred to as {{nihongo|O-Taga-san|お多賀さん}} by local residents.{{cite web |title=Taga Taisha official home page |url=http://www.tagataisya.or.jp/about/ |website=About Us |publisher=Taga Taisha}}{{in lang|ja}} The gardens of the inner ''[[shoin]]'', which date to the [[Momoyama period]] are a [[Cultural Properties of Japan|nationally designated]] [[Monuments of Japan|Place of Scenic Beauty]],{{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/401/1575 |archive-url=https://archive.today/20120803171529/https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/401/1575 |url-status=dead |archive-date=3 August 2012 |title=多賀神社奥書院庭園 |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |access-date=10 February 2012 }}{{Cite web|title=THE 10 BEST Things to Do Near Taga Taisha, Taga-cho - Tripadvisor|url=https://www.tripadvisor.com/AttractionsNear-g1121270-d1992498-Taga_Taisha-Taga_cho_Inukami_gun_Shiga_Prefecture_Kinki.html|access-date=2021-06-05|website=www.tripadvisor.com|language=en}} whereas the mid-[[Edo period]] ''Shoin'' itself is a Shiga Prefectural Important Cultural Property. ==Main ''kami''== * [[Izanagi]] * [[Izanami]] [[Category:Beppyo shrines]] [[Category:bgoodbye $1]] ==History== The foundation of Taga Taisha is unknown. It is mentioned in the ancient {{Lang|ja-latn|[[Kojiki]]}} and {{Lang|ja-latn|[[Nihon Shoki]]}} chronicles from 712 AD. The shrine's legend connects it with the legendary hero [[Yamato Takeru]], whose son, [[Inugami no Mitasuki]] was sent as an emissary to [[Japanese missions to Sui China|Sui China]] in 614 AD and the first [[Japanese missions to Tang China|Japanese embassy to Tang China]] in 630 AD. He was also the ancestor of the Inukami clan, from which [[Inukami District, Shiga]] (where the shrine is located) takes its name. However, in the 914 AD ''[[Engishiki]]'' records, it is listed only as a small shrine. From the [[Heian period]] it was revered as having efficiency for childbirth, vocational aid, fire extinguishing and longevity. During then [[Muromachi period]], as pilgrimages to the [[Ise Grand Shrine]] and the [[Kumano shrine|Kumano Sanzan]] gained in popularity, pilgrims were encouraged to visit Taga Shrine as part of the route. In the [[Sengoku period]], [[Toyotomi Hideyoshi]] had a strong faith in this shrine and frequently prayed for an extension of his mother's life. He donated 10,000 ''[[koku]]'', or the equivalent revenue of one year for a ''[[daimyō]]'' for its reconstruction and upkeep. The shrine was destroyed by a fire in 1615, but was rebuilt immediately by [[Shogun]] [[Tokugawa Hidetada]] and reconstructed in 1633 by [[Tokugawa Iemitsu]]. In 1651, [[Ii Naotaka]] of [[Hikone Domain]] granted it estates of 150 ''[[koku]]'' for its upkeep. However, much of the shrine was destroyed again by fire in 1733, and by a storm in 1791. Each time, it was rebuilt with the assistance of the [[Tokugawa shogunate]] and Hikone Domain. With the establishment of [[State Shinto]] following the [[Meiji restoration]], the shrine was designated a prefectural shrine under the [[Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines]] in 1871. It was promoted to a {{nihongo3|Imperial shrine, 2nd rank|官幣中社|[[Kanpei Chūsha]]}} in 1885 and to a {{nihongo3|Imperial shrine, 1st rank|官幣大社|Kanpei-taisha}} in 1914. Many of the shrine's buildings are Registered Tangible Cultural Properties of Taga Town.{{cite web |title=Registered Cultural Properties |url=https://www.town.taga.lg.jp/akebono/bunkazai/shitei.html |website=Taga Town Cultural Properties Center |publisher=Taga Town}}{{in lang|ja}} ==Festivals== *{{nihongo|'''Korei-taisai'''|古例大祭}}: Most important festival held on April 22. Also known as {{nihongo|'''Taga-matsuri'''|多賀まつり}}. *{{nihongo|'''Otaue-sai'''|御田植祭}}: A festival of planting rice with traditional rites held on June 7. *{{nihongo|'''Mantō-sai'''|万灯祭}}: A lantern festival held on the night of August 3–5. ==Specialty goods== *{{nihongo|'''Otaga-shakushi'''|お多賀杓子}}: A talisman ''shakushi'' (Japanese [[Ladle (spoon)|ladle]]) from the reign of [[Empress Genshō]]. Allegedly the origin of the Japanese word ''[[tadpole|otamajakushi]]''. *{{nihongo|'''Itokiri-mochi'''|糸切餅}}: A ''[[wagashi|Japanese sweet]]'' made of ''[[mochi]]'' sticky rice sold at souvenir shops around the shrine. ''Itokiri-mochi'' was first made to celebrate the [[Mongol invasions of Japan|Japanese victory over the Mongols]].{{Citation needed|date=November 2009}} ==Access== The shrine is about a 20 minute walk from [[Taga Taisha-mae Station]] on the [[Ohmi Railway]] [[Ohmi Railway Taga Line|Taga Line]], or about ten minutes by bus from [[Minami-Hikone Station]] on the [[West Japan Railway Company|JR West]] [[Biwako Line]]. ==See also== *[[Takamiya-juku]], the closest of the [[69 Stations of the Nakasendō]] to the shrine ==References== {{Reflist}} ==External links== {{Commons Category|Taga-taisha}} * [http://www.tagataisya.or.jp/ Official website] {{in lang|ja}} {{Shinto shrine}} [[Category:Pages that used to use Authority control]][[en:Taga-taisha]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Shiga Prefecture]] [[Category:Places of Scenic Beauty]] [[Category:Taga, Shiga]] [[Category:Ōmi Province]] [[Category:Kanpei Taisha]] [[en:Taga-taisha]][[Category:Shikinai Shosha]] [[Category:Taisha]] [[Category:Nagare-zukuri]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia 6]] [[en:Taga-taisha]]]] [[Category:mm]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia 6]] [[en:Taga-taisha]]]] [[Category:mm]] [[en:Taga-taisha]][[Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia 6]] [[en:Taga-taisha]]]] [[Category:mm]] [[Category:dd]][[Category:Taga-taisha]][[Category:dd]] [[Category:Bump-01-21]] [[Category:Bump-01-21]] [[Category:bump 1-26]] [[Category:second attempt at refreshing on January 27 2024]][[Category:Taga-taisha]][[Category:second attempt at refreshing on January 27 2024]] [[Category:kk]][[en:Taga-taisha]][[Category:kk]] [[Category:kk]][[en:Taga-taisha]][[Category:kk]] [[Category:qq]][[en:Taga-taisha]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:qq]][[en:Taga-taisha]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Taga-taisha]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:qqq]][[en:Taga-taisha]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:bump February 13, 2025 bump]][[en:Taga-taisha]][[Category:bump February 13, 2025 bump]] [[Category:Taga Shrines]] [[en:Taga-taisha]]{{Izanagi Faith}}

{{Use dmy dates|date=July 2019}} {{Infobox religious building | name = Taga-taisha
多賀大社 | image = Taga taisha1.JPG | caption = ''[[Honden]]'' of Taga-taisha | map_type = Japan Shiga Prefecture#Japan | coordinates = {{Coord|35|13|27|N|136|17|26|E|region:JP_scale:30000_source:jawiki|display=title,inline}} | map_relief = 1 | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | deity = [[Izanagi]], [[Izanami]] | established = Pre-[[Nara period]] | location = 604 Taga, Inukami-gun, Shiga-ken 522-0341 | website = {{URL|http://www.tagataisya.or.jp/}} | footnotes ={{box|background=white|align=center|wide=yes|border size=3px|border color=green|text align=center|[[Monuments of Japan|National Palace of Scenic Beauty]]}} }} [[File:Taga-taisha Haiden-b.jpg|thumb|280px|Haiden]] {{nihongo|'''Taga-taisha'''|多賀大社}} is a [[Shinto]] [[Jinja (Shinto)|shrine]] located in the town of [[Taga, Shiga|Taga]], [[Inukami District, Shiga|Inukami District]], [[Shiga Prefecture]], [[Japan]]. The shrine is frequently referred to as {{nihongo|O-Taga-san|お多賀さん}} by local residents.{{cite web |title=Taga Taisha official home page |url=http://www.tagataisya.or.jp/about/ |website=About Us |publisher=Taga Taisha}}{{in lang|ja}} The gardens of the inner ''[[shoin]]'', which date to the [[Momoyama period]] are a [[Cultural Properties of Japan|nationally designated]] [[Monuments of Japan|Place of Scenic Beauty]],{{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/401/1575 |archive-url=https://archive.today/20120803171529/https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/401/1575 |url-status=dead |archive-date=3 August 2012 |title=多賀神社奥書院庭園 |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |access-date=10 February 2012 }}{{Cite web|title=THE 10 BEST Things to Do Near Taga Taisha, Taga-cho - Tripadvisor|url=https://www.tripadvisor.com/AttractionsNear-g1121270-d1992498-Taga_Taisha-Taga_cho_Inukami_gun_Shiga_Prefecture_Kinki.html|access-date=2021-06-05|website=www.tripadvisor.com|language=en}} whereas the mid-[[Edo period]] ''Shoin'' itself is a Shiga Prefectural Important Cultural Property. ==Main ''kami''== * [[Izanagi]] * [[Izanami]] [[Category:Beppyo shrines]] [[Category:qq]]{{beppyo shrines}}]][[sn:Taga-taisha]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:qq]]{{Beppyo Shrines}}[[Category:qq]] [[Category:bgoodbye $1]] ==History== The foundation of Taga Taisha is unknown. It is mentioned in the ancient {{Lang|ja-latn|[[Kojiki]]}} and {{Lang|ja-latn|[[Nihon Shoki]]}} chronicles from 712 AD. The shrine's legend connects it with the legendary hero [[Yamato Takeru]], whose son, [[Inugami no Mitasuki]] was sent as an emissary to [[Japanese missions to Sui China|Sui China]] in 614 AD and the first [[Japanese missions to Tang China|Japanese embassy to Tang China]] in 630 AD. He was also the ancestor of the Inukami clan, from which [[Inukami District, Shiga]] (where the shrine is located) takes its name. However, in the 914 AD ''[[Engishiki]]'' records, it is listed only as a small shrine. From the [[Heian period]] it was revered as having efficiency for childbirth, vocational aid, fire extinguishing and longevity. During then [[Muromachi period]], as pilgrimages to the [[Ise Grand Shrine]] and the [[Kumano shrine|Kumano Sanzan]] gained in popularity, pilgrims were encouraged to visit Taga Shrine as part of the route. In the [[Sengoku period]], [[Toyotomi Hideyoshi]] had a strong faith in this shrine and frequently prayed for an extension of his mother's life. He donated 10,000 ''[[koku]]'', or the equivalent revenue of one year for a ''[[daimyō]]'' for its reconstruction and upkeep. The shrine was destroyed by a fire in 1615, but was rebuilt immediately by [[Shogun]] [[Tokugawa Hidetada]] and reconstructed in 1633 by [[Tokugawa Iemitsu]]. In 1651, [[Ii Naotaka]] of [[Hikone Domain]] granted it estates of 150 ''[[koku]]'' for its upkeep. However, much of the shrine was destroyed again by fire in 1733, and by a storm in 1791. Each time, it was rebuilt with the assistance of the [[Tokugawa shogunate]] and Hikone Domain. With the establishment of [[State Shinto]] following the [[Meiji restoration]], the shrine was designated a prefectural shrine under the [[Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines]] in 1871. It was promoted to a {{nihongo3|Imperial shrine, 2nd rank|官幣中社|[[Kanpei Chūsha]]}} in 1885 and to a {{nihongo3|Imperial shrine, 1st rank|官幣大社|Kanpei-taisha}} in 1914. Many of the shrine's buildings are Registered Tangible Cultural Properties of Taga Town.{{cite web |title=Registered Cultural Properties |url=https://www.town.taga.lg.jp/akebono/bunkazai/shitei.html |website=Taga Town Cultural Properties Center |publisher=Taga Town}}{{in lang|ja}} ==Festivals== *{{nihongo|'''Korei-taisai'''|古例大祭}}: Most important festival held on April 22. Also known as {{nihongo|'''Taga-matsuri'''|多賀まつり}}. *{{nihongo|'''Otaue-sai'''|御田植祭}}: A festival of planting rice with traditional rites held on June 7. *{{nihongo|'''Mantō-sai'''|万灯祭}}: A lantern festival held on the night of August 3–5. ==Specialty goods== *{{nihongo|'''Otaga-shakushi'''|お多賀杓子}}: A talisman ''shakushi'' (Japanese [[Ladle (spoon)|ladle]]) from the reign of [[Empress Genshō]]. Allegedly the origin of the Japanese word ''[[tadpole|otamajakushi]]''. *{{nihongo|'''Itokiri-mochi'''|糸切餅}}: A ''[[wagashi|Japanese sweet]]'' made of ''[[mochi]]'' sticky rice sold at souvenir shops around the shrine. ''Itokiri-mochi'' was first made to celebrate the [[Mongol invasions of Japan|Japanese victory over the Mongols]].{{Citation needed|date=November 2009}} ==Access== The shrine is about a 20 minute walk from [[Taga Taisha-mae Station]] on the [[Ohmi Railway]] [[Ohmi Railway Taga Line|Taga Line]], or about ten minutes by bus from [[Minami-Hikone Station]] on the [[West Japan Railway Company|JR West]] [[Biwako Line]]. ==See also== *[[Takamiya-juku]], the closest of the [[69 Stations of the Nakasendō]] to the shrine ==References== {{Reflist}} ==External links== {{Commons Category|Taga-taisha}} * {{Use dmy dates|date=July 2019}} {{Infobox religious building | name = Taga-taisha
多賀大社 | image = Taga taisha1.JPG | caption = ''[[Honden]]'' of Taga-taisha | map_type = Japan Shiga Prefecture#Japan | coordinates = {{Coord|35|13|27|N|136|17|26|E|region:JP_scale:30000_source:jawiki|display=title,inline}} | map_relief = 1 | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | deity = [[Izanagi]], [[Izanami]] | established = Pre-[[Nara period]] | location = 604 Taga, Inukami-gun, Shiga-ken 522-0341 | website = {{URL|http://www.tagataisya.or.jp/}} | footnotes ={{box|background=white|align=center|wide=yes|border size=3px|border color=green|text align=center|[[Monuments of Japan|National Palace of Scenic Beauty]]}} }} [[File:Taga-taisha Haiden-b.jpg|thumb|280px|Haiden]] {{nihongo|'''Taga-taisha'''|多賀大社}} is a [[Shinto]] [[Jinja (Shinto)|shrine]] located in the town of [[Taga, Shiga|Taga]], [[Inukami District, Shiga|Inukami District]], [[Shiga Prefecture]], [[Japan]]. The shrine is frequently referred to as {{nihongo|O-Taga-san|お多賀さん}} by local residents.{{cite web |title=Taga Taisha official home page |url=http://www.tagataisya.or.jp/about/ |website=About Us |publisher=Taga Taisha}}{{in lang|ja}} The gardens of the inner ''[[shoin]]'', which date to the [[Momoyama period]] are a [[Cultural Properties of Japan|nationally designated]] [[Monuments of Japan|Place of Scenic Beauty]],{{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/401/1575 |archive-url=https://archive.today/20120803171529/https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/401/1575 |url-status=dead |archive-date=3 August 2012 |title=多賀神社奥書院庭園 |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |access-date=10 February 2012 }}{{Cite web|title=THE 10 BEST Things to Do Near Taga Taisha, Taga-cho - Tripadvisor|url=https://www.tripadvisor.com/AttractionsNear-g1121270-d1992498-Taga_Taisha-Taga_cho_Inukami_gun_Shiga_Prefecture_Kinki.html|access-date=2021-06-05|website=www.tripadvisor.com|language=en}} whereas the mid-[[Edo period]] ''Shoin'' itself is a Shiga Prefectural Important Cultural Property. ==Main ''kami''== * [[Izanagi]] * [[Izanami]] [[Category:Beppyo shrines]] [[Category:qq]] [[sn:Taga-taisha]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:qq]] [[Category:qq]] [[Category:bgoodbye $1]] ==History== The foundation of Taga Taisha is unknown. It is mentioned in the ancient {{Lang|ja-latn|[[Kojiki]]}} and {{Lang|ja-latn|[[Nihon Shoki]]}} chronicles from 712 AD. The shrine's legend connects it with the legendary hero [[Yamato Takeru]], whose son, [[Inugami no Mitasuki]] was sent as an emissary to [[Japanese missions to Sui China|Sui China]] in 614 AD and the first [[Japanese missions to Tang China|Japanese embassy to Tang China]] in 630 AD. He was also the ancestor of the Inukami clan, from which [[Inukami District, Shiga]] (where the shrine is located) takes its name. However, in the 914 AD ''[[Engishiki]]'' records, it is listed only as a small shrine. From the [[Heian period]] it was revered as having efficiency for childbirth, vocational aid, fire extinguishing and longevity. During then [[Muromachi period]], as pilgrimages to the [[Ise Grand Shrine]] and the [[Kumano shrine|Kumano Sanzan]] gained in popularity, pilgrims were encouraged to visit Taga Shrine as part of the route. In the [[Sengoku period]], [[Toyotomi Hideyoshi]] had a strong faith in this shrine and frequently prayed for an extension of his mother's life. He donated 10,000 ''[[koku]]'', or the equivalent revenue of one year for a ''[[daimyō]]'' for its reconstruction and upkeep. The shrine was destroyed by a fire in 1615, but was rebuilt immediately by [[Shogun]] [[Tokugawa Hidetada]] and reconstructed in 1633 by [[Tokugawa Iemitsu]]. In 1651, [[Ii Naotaka]] of [[Hikone Domain]] granted it estates of 150 ''[[koku]]'' for its upkeep. However, much of the shrine was destroyed again by fire in 1733, and by a storm in 1791. Each time, it was rebuilt with the assistance of the [[Tokugawa shogunate]] and Hikone Domain. With the establishment of [[State Shinto]] following the [[Meiji restoration]], the shrine was designated a prefectural shrine under the [[Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines]] in 1871. It was promoted to a {{nihongo3|Imperial shrine, 2nd rank|官幣中社|[[Kanpei Chūsha]]}} in 1885 and to a {{nihongo3|Imperial shrine, 1st rank|官幣大社|Kanpei-taisha}} in 1914. Many of the shrine's buildings are Registered Tangible Cultural Properties of Taga Town.{{cite web |title=Registered Cultural Properties |url=https://www.town.taga.lg.jp/akebono/bunkazai/shitei.html |website=Taga Town Cultural Properties Center |publisher=Taga Town}}{{in lang|ja}} ==Festivals== *{{nihongo|'''Korei-taisai'''|古例大祭}}: Most important festival held on April 22. Also known as {{nihongo|'''Taga-matsuri'''|多賀まつり}}. *{{nihongo|'''Otaue-sai'''|御田植祭}}: A festival of planting rice with traditional rites held on June 7. *{{nihongo|'''Mantō-sai'''|万灯祭}}: A lantern festival held on the night of August 3–5. ==Specialty goods== *{{nihongo|'''Otaga-shakushi'''|お多賀杓子}}: A talisman ''shakushi'' (Japanese [[Ladle (spoon)|ladle]]) from the reign of [[Empress Genshō]]. Allegedly the origin of the Japanese word ''[[tadpole|otamajakushi]]''. *{{nihongo|'''Itokiri-mochi'''|糸切餅}}: A ''[[wagashi|Japanese sweet]]'' made of ''[[mochi]]'' sticky rice sold at souvenir shops around the shrine. ''Itokiri-mochi'' was first made to celebrate the [[Mongol invasions of Japan|Japanese victory over the Mongols]].{{Citation needed|date=November 2009}} ==Access== The shrine is about a 20 minute walk from [[Taga Taisha-mae Station]] on the [[Ohmi Railway]] [[Ohmi Railway Taga Line|Taga Line]], or about ten minutes by bus from [[Minami-Hikone Station]] on the [[West Japan Railway Company|JR West]] [[Biwako Line]]. ==See also== *[[Takamiya-juku]], the closest of the [[69 Stations of the Nakasendō]] to the shrine ==References== {{Reflist}} ==External links== {{Commons Category|Taga-taisha}} * 多賀大社 | image = Taga taisha1.JPG | caption = ''[[Honden]]'' of Taga-taisha | map_type = Japan Shiga Prefecture#Japan | coordinates = {{Coord|35|13|27|N|136|17|26|E|region:JP_scale:30000_source:jawiki|display=title,inline}} | map_relief = 1 | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | deity = [[Izanagi]], [[Izanami]] | established = Pre-[[Nara period]] | location = 604 Taga, Inukami-gun, Shiga-ken 522-0341 | website = {{URL|http://www.tagataisya.or.jp/}} | footnotes ={{box|background=white|align=center|wide=yes|border size=3px|border color=green|text align=center|[[Monuments of Japan|National Palace of Scenic Beauty]]}} }} [[File:Taga-taisha Haiden-b.jpg|thumb|280px|Haiden]] {{nihongo|'''Taga-taisha'''|多賀大社}} is a [[Shinto]] [[Jinja (Shinto)|shrine]] located in the town of [[Taga, Shiga|Taga]], [[Inukami District, Shiga|Inukami District]], [[Shiga Prefecture]], [[Japan]]. The shrine is frequently referred to as {{nihongo|O-Taga-san|お多賀さん}} by local residents.{{cite web |title=Taga Taisha official home page |url=http://www.tagataisya.or.jp/about/ |website=About Us |publisher=Taga Taisha}}{{in lang|ja}} The gardens of the inner ''[[shoin]]'', which date to the [[Momoyama period]] are a [[Cultural Properties of Japan|nationally designated]] [[Monuments of Japan|Place of Scenic Beauty]],{{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/401/1575 |archive-url=https://archive.today/20120803171529/https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/401/1575 |url-status=dead |archive-date=3 August 2012 |title=多賀神社奥書院庭園 |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |access-date=10 February 2012 }}{{Cite web|title=THE 10 BEST Things to Do Near Taga Taisha, Taga-cho - Tripadvisor|url=https://www.tripadvisor.com/AttractionsNear-g1121270-d1992498-Taga_Taisha-Taga_cho_Inukami_gun_Shiga_Prefecture_Kinki.html|access-date=2021-06-05|website=www.tripadvisor.com|language=en}} whereas the mid-[[Edo period]] ''Shoin'' itself is a Shiga Prefectural Important Cultural Property. ==Main ''kami''== * [[Izanagi]] * [[Izanami]] [[Category:Beppyo shrines]] [[Category:qq]] [[sn:Taga-taisha]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:qq]] [[Category:qq]] [[Category:bgoodbye $1]] ==History== The foundation of Taga Taisha is unknown. It is mentioned in the ancient {{Lang|ja-latn|[[Kojiki]]}} and {{Lang|ja-latn|[[Nihon Shoki]]}} chronicles from 712 AD. The shrine's legend connects it with the legendary hero [[Yamato Takeru]], whose son, [[Inugami no Mitasuki]] was sent as an emissary to [[Japanese missions to Sui China|Sui China]] in 614 AD and the first [[Japanese missions to Tang China|Japanese embassy to Tang China]] in 630 AD. He was also the ancestor of the Inukami clan, from which [[Inukami District, Shiga]] (where the shrine is located) takes its name. However, in the 914 AD ''[[Engishiki]]'' records, it is listed only as a small shrine. From the [[Heian period]] it was revered as having efficiency for childbirth, vocational aid, fire extinguishing and longevity. During then [[Muromachi period]], as pilgrimages to the [[Ise Grand Shrine]] and the [[Kumano shrine|Kumano Sanzan]] gained in popularity, pilgrims were encouraged to visit Taga Shrine as part of the route. In the [[Sengoku period]], [[Toyotomi Hideyoshi]] had a strong faith in this shrine and frequently prayed for an extension of his mother's life. He donated 10,000 ''[[koku]]'', or the equivalent revenue of one year for a ''[[daimyō]]'' for its reconstruction and upkeep. The shrine was destroyed by a fire in 1615, but was rebuilt immediately by [[Shogun]] [[Tokugawa Hidetada]] and reconstructed in 1633 by [[Tokugawa Iemitsu]]. In 1651, [[Ii Naotaka]] of [[Hikone Domain]] granted it estates of 150 ''[[koku]]'' for its upkeep. However, much of the shrine was destroyed again by fire in 1733, and by a storm in 1791. Each time, it was rebuilt with the assistance of the [[Tokugawa shogunate]] and Hikone Domain. With the establishment of [[State Shinto]] following the [[Meiji restoration]], the shrine was designated a prefectural shrine under the [[Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines]] in 1871. It was promoted to a {{nihongo3|Imperial shrine, 2nd rank|官幣中社|[[Kanpei Chūsha]]}} in 1885 and to a {{nihongo3|Imperial shrine, 1st rank|官幣大社|Kanpei-taisha}} in 1914. Many of the shrine's buildings are Registered Tangible Cultural Properties of Taga Town.{{cite web |title=Registered Cultural Properties |url=https://www.town.taga.lg.jp/akebono/bunkazai/shitei.html |website=Taga Town Cultural Properties Center |publisher=Taga Town}}{{in lang|ja}} ==Festivals== *{{nihongo|'''Korei-taisai'''|古例大祭}}: Most important festival held on April 22. Also known as {{nihongo|'''Taga-matsuri'''|多賀まつり}}. *{{nihongo|'''Otaue-sai'''|御田植祭}}: A festival of planting rice with traditional rites held on June 7. *{{nihongo|'''Mantō-sai'''|万灯祭}}: A lantern festival held on the night of August 3–5. ==Specialty goods== *{{nihongo|'''Otaga-shakushi'''|お多賀杓子}}: A talisman ''shakushi'' (Japanese [[Ladle (spoon)|ladle]]) from the reign of [[Empress Genshō]]. Allegedly the origin of the Japanese word ''[[tadpole|otamajakushi]]''. *{{nihongo|'''Itokiri-mochi'''|糸切餅}}: A ''[[wagashi|Japanese sweet]]'' made of ''[[mochi]]'' sticky rice sold at souvenir shops around the shrine. ''Itokiri-mochi'' was first made to celebrate the [[Mongol invasions of Japan|Japanese victory over the Mongols]].{{Citation needed|date=November 2009}} ==Access== The shrine is about a 20 minute walk from [[Taga Taisha-mae Station]] on the [[Ohmi Railway]] [[Ohmi Railway Taga Line|Taga Line]], or about ten minutes by bus from [[Minami-Hikone Station]] on the [[West Japan Railway Company|JR West]] [[Biwako Line]]. ==See also== *[[Takamiya-juku]], the closest of the [[69 Stations of the Nakasendō]] to the shrine ==References== {{Reflist}} ==External links== {{Commons Category|Taga-taisha}} * 多賀大社 | image = Taga taisha1.JPG | caption = ''[[Honden]]'' of Taga-taisha | map_type = Japan Shiga Prefecture#Japan | coordinates = {{Coord|35|13|27|N|136|17|26|E|region:JP_scale:30000_source:jawiki|display=title,inline}} | map_relief = 1 | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | deity = [[Izanagi]], [[Izanami]] | established = Pre-[[Nara period]] | location = 604 Taga, Inukami-gun, Shiga-ken 522-0341 | website = {{URL|http://www.tagataisya.or.jp/}} | footnotes ={{box|background=white|align=center|wide=yes|border size=3px|border color=green|text align=center|[[Monuments of Japan|National Palace of Scenic Beauty]]}} }} [[File:Taga-taisha Haiden-b.jpg|thumb|280px|Haiden]] {{nihongo|'''Taga-taisha'''|多賀大社}} is a [[Shinto]] [[Jinja (Shinto)|shrine]] located in the town of [[Taga, Shiga|Taga]], [[Inukami District, Shiga|Inukami District]], [[Shiga Prefecture]], [[Japan]]. The shrine is frequently referred to as {{nihongo|O-Taga-san|お多賀さん}} by local residents.{{cite web |title=Taga Taisha official home page |url=http://www.tagataisya.or.jp/about/ |website=About Us |publisher=Taga Taisha}}{{in lang|ja}} The gardens of the inner ''[[shoin]]'', which date to the [[Momoyama period]] are a [[Cultural Properties of Japan|nationally designated]] [[Monuments of Japan|Place of Scenic Beauty]],{{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/401/1575 |archive-url=https://archive.today/20120803171529/https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/401/1575 |url-status=dead |archive-date=3 August 2012 |title=多賀神社奥書院庭園 |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |access-date=10 February 2012 }}{{Cite web|title=THE 10 BEST Things to Do Near Taga Taisha, Taga-cho - Tripadvisor|url=https://www.tripadvisor.com/AttractionsNear-g1121270-d1992498-Taga_Taisha-Taga_cho_Inukami_gun_Shiga_Prefecture_Kinki.html|access-date=2021-06-05|website=www.tripadvisor.com|language=en}} whereas the mid-[[Edo period]] ''Shoin'' itself is a Shiga Prefectural Important Cultural Property. ==Main ''kami''== * [[Izanagi]] * [[Izanami]] [[Category:Beppyo shrines]] [[Category:qq]] [[sn:Taga-taisha]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:qq]] [[Category:qq]] [[Category:bgoodbye $1]] ==History== The foundation of Taga Taisha is unknown. It is mentioned in the ancient {{Lang|ja-latn|[[Kojiki]]}} and {{Lang|ja-latn|[[Nihon Shoki]]}} chronicles from 712 AD. The shrine's legend connects it with the legendary hero [[Yamato Takeru]], whose son, [[Inugami no Mitasuki]] was sent as an emissary to [[Japanese missions to Sui China|Sui China]] in 614 AD and the first [[Japanese missions to Tang China|Japanese embassy to Tang China]] in 630 AD. He was also the ancestor of the Inukami clan, from which [[Inukami District, Shiga]] (where the shrine is located) takes its name. However, in the 914 AD ''[[Engishiki]]'' records, it is listed only as a small shrine. From the [[Heian period]] it was revered as having efficiency for childbirth, vocational aid, fire extinguishing and longevity. During then [[Muromachi period]], as pilgrimages to the [[Ise Grand Shrine]] and the [[Kumano shrine|Kumano Sanzan]] gained in popularity, pilgrims were encouraged to visit Taga Shrine as part of the route. In the [[Sengoku period]], [[Toyotomi Hideyoshi]] had a strong faith in this shrine and frequently prayed for an extension of his mother's life. He donated 10,000 ''[[koku]]'', or the equivalent revenue of one year for a ''[[daimyō]]'' for its reconstruction and upkeep. The shrine was destroyed by a fire in 1615, but was rebuilt immediately by [[Shogun]] [[Tokugawa Hidetada]] and reconstructed in 1633 by [[Tokugawa Iemitsu]]. In 1651, [[Ii Naotaka]] of [[Hikone Domain]] granted it estates of 150 ''[[koku]]'' for its upkeep. However, much of the shrine was destroyed again by fire in 1733, and by a storm in 1791. Each time, it was rebuilt with the assistance of the [[Tokugawa shogunate]] and Hikone Domain. With the establishment of [[State Shinto]] following the [[Meiji restoration]], the shrine was designated a prefectural shrine under the [[Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines]] in 1871. It was promoted to a {{nihongo3|Imperial shrine, 2nd rank|官幣中社|[[Kanpei Chūsha]]}} in 1885 and to a {{nihongo3|Imperial shrine, 1st rank|官幣大社|Kanpei-taisha}} in 1914. Many of the shrine's buildings are Registered Tangible Cultural Properties of Taga Town.{{cite web |title=Registered Cultural Properties |url=https://www.town.taga.lg.jp/akebono/bunkazai/shitei.html |website=Taga Town Cultural Properties Center |publisher=Taga Town}}{{in lang|ja}} ==Festivals== *{{nihongo|'''Korei-taisai'''|古例大祭}}: Most important festival held on April 22. Also known as {{nihongo|'''Taga-matsuri'''|多賀まつり}}. *{{nihongo|'''Otaue-sai'''|御田植祭}}: A festival of planting rice with traditional rites held on June 7. *{{nihongo|'''Mantō-sai'''|万灯祭}}: A lantern festival held on the night of August 3–5. ==Specialty goods== *{{nihongo|'''Otaga-shakushi'''|お多賀杓子}}: A talisman ''shakushi'' (Japanese [[Ladle (spoon)|ladle]]) from the reign of [[Empress Genshō]]. Allegedly the origin of the Japanese word ''[[tadpole|otamajakushi]]''. *{{nihongo|'''Itokiri-mochi'''|糸切餅}}: A ''[[wagashi|Japanese sweet]]'' made of ''[[mochi]]'' sticky rice sold at souvenir shops around the shrine. ''Itokiri-mochi'' was first made to celebrate the [[Mongol invasions of Japan|Japanese victory over the Mongols]].{{Citation needed|date=November 2009}} ==Access== The shrine is about a 20 minute walk from [[Taga Taisha-mae Station]] on the [[Ohmi Railway]] [[Ohmi Railway Taga Line|Taga Line]], or about ten minutes by bus from [[Minami-Hikone Station]] on the [[West Japan Railway Company|JR West]] [[Biwako Line]]. ==See also== *[[Takamiya-juku]], the closest of the [[69 Stations of the Nakasendō]] to the shrine ==References== {{Reflist}} ==External links== {{Commons Category|Taga-taisha}} * {{Use dmy dates|date=July 2019}} {{Infobox religious building | name = Taga-taisha
多賀大社 | image = Taga taisha1.JPG | caption = ''[[Honden]]'' of Taga-taisha | map_type = Japan Shiga Prefecture#Japan | coordinates = {{Coord|35|13|27|N|136|17|26|E|region:JP_scale:30000_source:jawiki|display=title,inline}} | map_relief = 1 | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | deity = [[Izanagi]], [[Izanami]] | established = Pre-[[Nara period]] | location = 604 Taga, Inukami-gun, Shiga-ken 522-0341 | website = {{URL|http://www.tagataisya.or.jp/}} | footnotes ={{box|background=white|align=center|wide=yes|border size=3px|border color=green|text align=center|[[Monuments of Japan|National Palace of Scenic Beauty]]}} }} [[File:Taga-taisha Haiden-b.jpg|thumb|280px|Haiden]] {{nihongo|'''Taga-taisha'''|多賀大社}} is a [[Shinto]] [[Jinja (Shinto)|shrine]] located in the town of [[Taga, Shiga|Taga]], [[Inukami District, Shiga|Inukami District]], [[Shiga Prefecture]], [[Japan]]. The shrine is frequently referred to as {{nihongo|O-Taga-san|お多賀さん}} by local residents.{{cite web |title=Taga Taisha official home page |url=http://www.tagataisya.or.jp/about/ |website=About Us |publisher=Taga Taisha}}{{in lang|ja}} The gardens of the inner ''[[shoin]]'', which date to the [[Momoyama period]] are a [[Cultural Properties of Japan|nationally designated]] [[Monuments of Japan|Place of Scenic Beauty]],{{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/401/1575 |archive-url=https://archive.today/20120803171529/https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/401/1575 |url-status=dead |archive-date=3 August 2012 |title=多賀神社奥書院庭園 |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |access-date=10 February 2012 }}{{Cite web|title=THE 10 BEST Things to Do Near Taga Taisha, Taga-cho - Tripadvisor|url=https://www.tripadvisor.com/AttractionsNear-g1121270-d1992498-Taga_Taisha-Taga_cho_Inukami_gun_Shiga_Prefecture_Kinki.html|access-date=2021-06-05|website=www.tripadvisor.com|language=en}} whereas the mid-[[Edo period]] ''Shoin'' itself is a Shiga Prefectural Important Cultural Property. ==Main ''kami''== * [[Izanagi]] * [[Izanami]] [[Category:Beppyo shrines]] [[Category:qq]] [[sn:Taga-taisha]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:qq]] [[Category:qq]] [[Category:bgoodbye $1]] ==History== The foundation of Taga Taisha is unknown. It is mentioned in the ancient {{Lang|ja-latn|[[Kojiki]]}} and {{Lang|ja-latn|[[Nihon Shoki]]}} chronicles from 712 AD. The shrine's legend connects it with the legendary hero [[Yamato Takeru]], whose son, [[Inugami no Mitasuki]] was sent as an emissary to [[Japanese missions to Sui China|Sui China]] in 614 AD and the first [[Japanese missions to Tang China|Japanese embassy to Tang China]] in 630 AD. He was also the ancestor of the Inukami clan, from which [[Inukami District, Shiga]] (where the shrine is located) takes its name. However, in the 914 AD ''[[Engishiki]]'' records, it is listed only as a small shrine. From the [[Heian period]] it was revered as having efficiency for childbirth, vocational aid, fire extinguishing and longevity. During then [[Muromachi period]], as pilgrimages to the [[Ise Grand Shrine]] and the [[Kumano shrine|Kumano Sanzan]] gained in popularity, pilgrims were encouraged to visit Taga Shrine as part of the route. In the [[Sengoku period]], [[Toyotomi Hideyoshi]] had a strong faith in this shrine and frequently prayed for an extension of his mother's life. He donated 10,000 ''[[koku]]'', or the equivalent revenue of one year for a ''[[daimyō]]'' for its reconstruction and upkeep. The shrine was destroyed by a fire in 1615, but was rebuilt immediately by [[Shogun]] [[Tokugawa Hidetada]] and reconstructed in 1633 by [[Tokugawa Iemitsu]]. In 1651, [[Ii Naotaka]] of [[Hikone Domain]] granted it estates of 150 ''[[koku]]'' for its upkeep. However, much of the shrine was destroyed again by fire in 1733, and by a storm in 1791. Each time, it was rebuilt with the assistance of the [[Tokugawa shogunate]] and Hikone Domain. With the establishment of [[State Shinto]] following the [[Meiji restoration]], the shrine was designated a prefectural shrine under the [[Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines]] in 1871. It was promoted to a {{nihongo3|Imperial shrine, 2nd rank|官幣中社|[[Kanpei Chūsha]]}} in 1885 and to a {{nihongo3|Imperial shrine, 1st rank|官幣大社|Kanpei-taisha}} in 1914. Many of the shrine's buildings are Registered Tangible Cultural Properties of Taga Town.{{cite web |title=Registered Cultural Properties |url=https://www.town.taga.lg.jp/akebono/bunkazai/shitei.html |website=Taga Town Cultural Properties Center |publisher=Taga Town}}{{in lang|ja}} ==Festivals== *{{nihongo|'''Korei-taisai'''|古例大祭}}: Most important festival held on April 22. Also known as {{nihongo|'''Taga-matsuri'''|多賀まつり}}. *{{nihongo|'''Otaue-sai'''|御田植祭}}: A festival of planting rice with traditional rites held on June 7. *{{nihongo|'''Mantō-sai'''|万灯祭}}: A lantern festival held on the night of August 3–5. ==Specialty goods== *{{nihongo|'''Otaga-shakushi'''|お多賀杓子}}: A talisman ''shakushi'' (Japanese [[Ladle (spoon)|ladle]]) from the reign of [[Empress Genshō]]. Allegedly the origin of the Japanese word ''[[tadpole|otamajakushi]]''. *{{nihongo|'''Itokiri-mochi'''|糸切餅}}: A ''[[wagashi|Japanese sweet]]'' made of ''[[mochi]]'' sticky rice sold at souvenir shops around the shrine. ''Itokiri-mochi'' was first made to celebrate the [[Mongol invasions of Japan|Japanese victory over the Mongols]].{{Citation needed|date=November 2009}} ==Access== The shrine is about a 20 minute walk from [[Taga Taisha-mae Station]] on the [[Ohmi Railway]] [[Ohmi Railway Taga Line|Taga Line]], or about ten minutes by bus from [[Minami-Hikone Station]] on the [[West Japan Railway Company|JR West]] [[Biwako Line]]. ==See also== *[[Takamiya-juku]], the closest of the [[69 Stations of the Nakasendō]] to the shrine ==References== {{Reflist}} ==External links== {{Commons Category|Taga-taisha}} * [http://www.tagataisya.or.jp/ Official website] {{in lang|ja}} {{Shinto shrine}} [[Category:Pages that used to use Authority control]][[en:Taga-taisha]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Shiga Prefecture]] {{Use dmy dates|date=July 2019}} {{Infobox religious building | name = Taga-taisha
多賀大社 | image = Taga taisha1.JPG | caption = ''[[Honden]]'' of Taga-taisha | map_type = Japan Shiga Prefecture#Japan | coordinates = {{Coord|35|13|27|N|136|17|26|E|region:JP_scale:30000_source:jawiki|display=title,inline}} | map_relief = 1 | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | deity = [[Izanagi]], [[Izanami]] | established = Pre-[[Nara period]] | location = 604 Taga, Inukami-gun, Shiga-ken 522-0341 | website = {{URL|http://www.tagataisya.or.jp/}} | footnotes ={{box|background=white|align=center|wide=yes|border size=3px|border color=green|text align=center|[[Monuments of Japan|National Palace of Scenic Beauty]]}} }} [[File:Taga-taisha Haiden-b.jpg|thumb|280px|Haiden]] {{nihongo|'''Taga-taisha'''|多賀大社}} is a [[Shinto]] [[Jinja (Shinto)|shrine]] located in the town of [[Taga, Shiga|Taga]], [[Inukami District, Shiga|Inukami District]], [[Shiga Prefecture]], [[Japan]]. The shrine is frequently referred to as {{nihongo|O-Taga-san|お多賀さん}} by local residents.{{cite web |title=Taga Taisha official home page |url=http://www.tagataisya.or.jp/about/ |website=About Us |publisher=Taga Taisha}}{{in lang|ja}} The gardens of the inner ''[[shoin]]'', which date to the [[Momoyama period]] are a [[Cultural Properties of Japan|nationally designated]] [[Monuments of Japan|Place of Scenic Beauty]],{{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/401/1575 |archive-url=https://archive.today/20120803171529/https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/401/1575 |url-status=dead |archive-date=3 August 2012 |title=多賀神社奥書院庭園 |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |access-date=10 February 2012 }}{{Cite web|title=THE 10 BEST Things to Do Near Taga Taisha, Taga-cho - Tripadvisor|url=https://www.tripadvisor.com/AttractionsNear-g1121270-d1992498-Taga_Taisha-Taga_cho_Inukami_gun_Shiga_Prefecture_Kinki.html|access-date=2021-06-05|website=www.tripadvisor.com|language=en}} whereas the mid-[[Edo period]] ''Shoin'' itself is a Shiga Prefectural Important Cultural Property. ==Main ''kami''== * [[Izanagi]] * [[Izanami]] [[Category:Beppyo shrines]] [[Category:qq]] [[sn:Taga-taisha]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:qq]] [[Category:qq]] [[Category:bgoodbye $1]] ==History== The foundation of Taga Taisha is unknown. It is mentioned in the ancient {{Lang|ja-latn|[[Kojiki]]}} and {{Lang|ja-latn|[[Nihon Shoki]]}} chronicles from 712 AD. The shrine's legend connects it with the legendary hero [[Yamato Takeru]], whose son, [[Inugami no Mitasuki]] was sent as an emissary to [[Japanese missions to Sui China|Sui China]] in 614 AD and the first [[Japanese missions to Tang China|Japanese embassy to Tang China]] in 630 AD. He was also the ancestor of the Inukami clan, from which [[Inukami District, Shiga]] (where the shrine is located) takes its name. However, in the 914 AD ''[[Engishiki]]'' records, it is listed only as a small shrine. From the [[Heian period]] it was revered as having efficiency for childbirth, vocational aid, fire extinguishing and longevity. During then [[Muromachi period]], as pilgrimages to the [[Ise Grand Shrine]] and the [[Kumano shrine|Kumano Sanzan]] gained in popularity, pilgrims were encouraged to visit Taga Shrine as part of the route. In the [[Sengoku period]], [[Toyotomi Hideyoshi]] had a strong faith in this shrine and frequently prayed for an extension of his mother's life. He donated 10,000 ''[[koku]]'', or the equivalent revenue of one year for a ''[[daimyō]]'' for its reconstruction and upkeep. The shrine was destroyed by a fire in 1615, but was rebuilt immediately by [[Shogun]] [[Tokugawa Hidetada]] and reconstructed in 1633 by [[Tokugawa Iemitsu]]. In 1651, [[Ii Naotaka]] of [[Hikone Domain]] granted it estates of 150 ''[[koku]]'' for its upkeep. However, much of the shrine was destroyed again by fire in 1733, and by a storm in 1791. Each time, it was rebuilt with the assistance of the [[Tokugawa shogunate]] and Hikone Domain. With the establishment of [[State Shinto]] following the [[Meiji restoration]], the shrine was designated a prefectural shrine under the [[Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines]] in 1871. It was promoted to a {{nihongo3|Imperial shrine, 2nd rank|官幣中社|[[Kanpei Chūsha]]}} in 1885 and to a {{nihongo3|Imperial shrine, 1st rank|官幣大社|Kanpei-taisha}} in 1914. Many of the shrine's buildings are Registered Tangible Cultural Properties of Taga Town.{{cite web |title=Registered Cultural Properties |url=https://www.town.taga.lg.jp/akebono/bunkazai/shitei.html |website=Taga Town Cultural Properties Center |publisher=Taga Town}}{{in lang|ja}} ==Festivals== *{{nihongo|'''Korei-taisai'''|古例大祭}}: Most important festival held on April 22. Also known as {{nihongo|'''Taga-matsuri'''|多賀まつり}}. *{{nihongo|'''Otaue-sai'''|御田植祭}}: A festival of planting rice with traditional rites held on June 7. *{{nihongo|'''Mantō-sai'''|万灯祭}}: A lantern festival held on the night of August 3–5. ==Specialty goods== *{{nihongo|'''Otaga-shakushi'''|お多賀杓子}}: A talisman ''shakushi'' (Japanese [[Ladle (spoon)|ladle]]) from the reign of [[Empress Genshō]]. Allegedly the origin of the Japanese word ''[[tadpole|otamajakushi]]''. *{{nihongo|'''Itokiri-mochi'''|糸切餅}}: A ''[[wagashi|Japanese sweet]]'' made of ''[[mochi]]'' sticky rice sold at souvenir shops around the shrine. ''Itokiri-mochi'' was first made to celebrate the [[Mongol invasions of Japan|Japanese victory over the Mongols]].{{Citation needed|date=November 2009}} ==Access== The shrine is about a 20 minute walk from [[Taga Taisha-mae Station]] on the [[Ohmi Railway]] [[Ohmi Railway Taga Line|Taga Line]], or about ten minutes by bus from [[Minami-Hikone Station]] on the [[West Japan Railway Company|JR West]] [[Biwako Line]]. ==See also== *[[Takamiya-juku]], the closest of the [[69 Stations of the Nakasendō]] to the shrine ==References== {{Reflist}} ==External links== {{Commons Category|Taga-taisha}} * {{Use dmy dates|date=July 2019}} {{Infobox religious building | name = Taga-taisha
多賀大社 | image = Taga taisha1.JPG | caption = ''[[Honden]]'' of Taga-taisha | map_type = Japan Shiga Prefecture#Japan | coordinates = {{Coord|35|13|27|N|136|17|26|E|region:JP_scale:30000_source:jawiki|display=title,inline}} | map_relief = 1 | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | deity = [[Izanagi]], [[Izanami]] | established = Pre-[[Nara period]] | location = 604 Taga, Inukami-gun, Shiga-ken 522-0341 | website = {{URL|http://www.tagataisya.or.jp/}} | footnotes ={{box|background=white|align=center|wide=yes|border size=3px|border color=green|text align=center|[[Monuments of Japan|National Palace of Scenic Beauty]]}} }} [[File:Taga-taisha Haiden-b.jpg|thumb|280px|Haiden]] {{nihongo|'''Taga-taisha'''|多賀大社}} is a [[Shinto]] [[Jinja (Shinto)|shrine]] located in the town of [[Taga, Shiga|Taga]], [[Inukami District, Shiga|Inukami District]], [[Shiga Prefecture]], [[Japan]]. The shrine is frequently referred to as {{nihongo|O-Taga-san|お多賀さん}} by local residents.{{cite web |title=Taga Taisha official home page |url=http://www.tagataisya.or.jp/about/ |website=About Us |publisher=Taga Taisha}}{{in lang|ja}} The gardens of the inner ''[[shoin]]'', which date to the [[Momoyama period]] are a [[Cultural Properties of Japan|nationally designated]] [[Monuments of Japan|Place of Scenic Beauty]],{{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/401/1575 |archive-url=https://archive.today/20120803171529/https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/401/1575 |url-status=dead |archive-date=3 August 2012 |title=多賀神社奥書院庭園 |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |access-date=10 February 2012 }}{{Cite web|title=THE 10 BEST Things to Do Near Taga Taisha, Taga-cho - Tripadvisor|url=https://www.tripadvisor.com/AttractionsNear-g1121270-d1992498-Taga_Taisha-Taga_cho_Inukami_gun_Shiga_Prefecture_Kinki.html|access-date=2021-06-05|website=www.tripadvisor.com|language=en}} whereas the mid-[[Edo period]] ''Shoin'' itself is a Shiga Prefectural Important Cultural Property. ==Main ''kami''== * [[Izanagi]] * [[Izanami]] [[Category:Beppyo shrines]] [[Category:qq]] [[sn:Taga-taisha]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:qq]] [[Category:qq]] [[Category:bgoodbye $1]] ==History== The foundation of Taga Taisha is unknown. It is mentioned in the ancient {{Lang|ja-latn|[[Kojiki]]}} and {{Lang|ja-latn|[[Nihon Shoki]]}} chronicles from 712 AD. The shrine's legend connects it with the legendary hero [[Yamato Takeru]], whose son, [[Inugami no Mitasuki]] was sent as an emissary to [[Japanese missions to Sui China|Sui China]] in 614 AD and the first [[Japanese missions to Tang China|Japanese embassy to Tang China]] in 630 AD. He was also the ancestor of the Inukami clan, from which [[Inukami District, Shiga]] (where the shrine is located) takes its name. However, in the 914 AD ''[[Engishiki]]'' records, it is listed only as a small shrine. From the [[Heian period]] it was revered as having efficiency for childbirth, vocational aid, fire extinguishing and longevity. During then [[Muromachi period]], as pilgrimages to the [[Ise Grand Shrine]] and the [[Kumano shrine|Kumano Sanzan]] gained in popularity, pilgrims were encouraged to visit Taga Shrine as part of the route. In the [[Sengoku period]], [[Toyotomi Hideyoshi]] had a strong faith in this shrine and frequently prayed for an extension of his mother's life. He donated 10,000 ''[[koku]]'', or the equivalent revenue of one year for a ''[[daimyō]]'' for its reconstruction and upkeep. The shrine was destroyed by a fire in 1615, but was rebuilt immediately by [[Shogun]] [[Tokugawa Hidetada]] and reconstructed in 1633 by [[Tokugawa Iemitsu]]. In 1651, [[Ii Naotaka]] of [[Hikone Domain]] granted it estates of 150 ''[[koku]]'' for its upkeep. However, much of the shrine was destroyed again by fire in 1733, and by a storm in 1791. Each time, it was rebuilt with the assistance of the [[Tokugawa shogunate]] and Hikone Domain. With the establishment of [[State Shinto]] following the [[Meiji restoration]], the shrine was designated a prefectural shrine under the [[Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines]] in 1871. It was promoted to a {{nihongo3|Imperial shrine, 2nd rank|官幣中社|[[Kanpei Chūsha]]}} in 1885 and to a {{nihongo3|Imperial shrine, 1st rank|官幣大社|Kanpei-taisha}} in 1914. Many of the shrine's buildings are Registered Tangible Cultural Properties of Taga Town.{{cite web |title=Registered Cultural Properties |url=https://www.town.taga.lg.jp/akebono/bunkazai/shitei.html |website=Taga Town Cultural Properties Center |publisher=Taga Town}}{{in lang|ja}} ==Festivals== *{{nihongo|'''Korei-taisai'''|古例大祭}}: Most important festival held on April 22. Also known as {{nihongo|'''Taga-matsuri'''|多賀まつり}}. *{{nihongo|'''Otaue-sai'''|御田植祭}}: A festival of planting rice with traditional rites held on June 7. *{{nihongo|'''Mantō-sai'''|万灯祭}}: A lantern festival held on the night of August 3–5. ==Specialty goods== *{{nihongo|'''Otaga-shakushi'''|お多賀杓子}}: A talisman ''shakushi'' (Japanese [[Ladle (spoon)|ladle]]) from the reign of [[Empress Genshō]]. Allegedly the origin of the Japanese word ''[[tadpole|otamajakushi]]''. *{{nihongo|'''Itokiri-mochi'''|糸切餅}}: A ''[[wagashi|Japanese sweet]]'' made of ''[[mochi]]'' sticky rice sold at souvenir shops around the shrine. ''Itokiri-mochi'' was first made to celebrate the [[Mongol invasions of Japan|Japanese victory over the Mongols]].{{Citation needed|date=November 2009}} ==Access== The shrine is about a 20 minute walk from [[Taga Taisha-mae Station]] on the [[Ohmi Railway]] [[Ohmi Railway Taga Line|Taga Line]], or about ten minutes by bus from [[Minami-Hikone Station]] on the [[West Japan Railway Company|JR West]] [[Biwako Line]]. ==See also== *[[Takamiya-juku]], the closest of the [[69 Stations of the Nakasendō]] to the shrine ==References== {{Reflist}} ==External links== {{Commons Category|Taga-taisha}} * {{Use dmy dates|date=July 2019}} {{Infobox religious building | name = Taga-taisha
多賀大社 | image = Taga taisha1.JPG | caption = ''[[Honden]]'' of Taga-taisha | map_type = Japan Shiga Prefecture#Japan | coordinates = {{Coord|35|13|27|N|136|17|26|E|region:JP_scale:30000_source:jawiki|display=title,inline}} | map_relief = 1 | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | deity = [[Izanagi]], [[Izanami]] | established = Pre-[[Nara period]] | location = 604 Taga, Inukami-gun, Shiga-ken 522-0341 | website = {{URL|http://www.tagataisya.or.jp/}} | footnotes ={{box|background=white|align=center|wide=yes|border size=3px|border color=green|text align=center|[[Monuments of Japan|National Palace of Scenic Beauty]]}} }} [[File:Taga-taisha Haiden-b.jpg|thumb|280px|Haiden]] {{nihongo|'''Taga-taisha'''|多賀大社}} is a [[Shinto]] [[Jinja (Shinto)|shrine]] located in the town of [[Taga, Shiga|Taga]], [[Inukami District, Shiga|Inukami District]], [[Shiga Prefecture]], [[Japan]]. The shrine is frequently referred to as {{nihongo|O-Taga-san|お多賀さん}} by local residents.{{cite web |title=Taga Taisha official home page |url=http://www.tagataisya.or.jp/about/ |website=About Us |publisher=Taga Taisha}}{{in lang|ja}} The gardens of the inner ''[[shoin]]'', which date to the [[Momoyama period]] are a [[Cultural Properties of Japan|nationally designated]] [[Monuments of Japan|Place of Scenic Beauty]],{{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/401/1575 |archive-url=https://archive.today/20120803171529/https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/401/1575 |url-status=dead |archive-date=3 August 2012 |title=多賀神社奥書院庭園 |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |access-date=10 February 2012 }}{{Cite web|title=THE 10 BEST Things to Do Near Taga Taisha, Taga-cho - Tripadvisor|url=https://www.tripadvisor.com/AttractionsNear-g1121270-d1992498-Taga_Taisha-Taga_cho_Inukami_gun_Shiga_Prefecture_Kinki.html|access-date=2021-06-05|website=www.tripadvisor.com|language=en}} whereas the mid-[[Edo period]] ''Shoin'' itself is a Shiga Prefectural Important Cultural Property. ==Main ''kami''== * [[Izanagi]] * [[Izanami]] [[Category:Beppyo shrines]] [[Category:qq]] [[sn:Taga-taisha]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:qq]] [[Category:qq]] [[Category:bgoodbye $1]] ==History== The foundation of Taga Taisha is unknown. It is mentioned in the ancient {{Lang|ja-latn|[[Kojiki]]}} and {{Lang|ja-latn|[[Nihon Shoki]]}} chronicles from 712 AD. The shrine's legend connects it with the legendary hero [[Yamato Takeru]], whose son, [[Inugami no Mitasuki]] was sent as an emissary to [[Japanese missions to Sui China|Sui China]] in 614 AD and the first [[Japanese missions to Tang China|Japanese embassy to Tang China]] in 630 AD. He was also the ancestor of the Inukami clan, from which [[Inukami District, Shiga]] (where the shrine is located) takes its name. However, in the 914 AD ''[[Engishiki]]'' records, it is listed only as a small shrine. From the [[Heian period]] it was revered as having efficiency for childbirth, vocational aid, fire extinguishing and longevity. During then [[Muromachi period]], as pilgrimages to the [[Ise Grand Shrine]] and the [[Kumano shrine|Kumano Sanzan]] gained in popularity, pilgrims were encouraged to visit Taga Shrine as part of the route. In the [[Sengoku period]], [[Toyotomi Hideyoshi]] had a strong faith in this shrine and frequently prayed for an extension of his mother's life. He donated 10,000 ''[[koku]]'', or the equivalent revenue of one year for a ''[[daimyō]]'' for its reconstruction and upkeep. The shrine was destroyed by a fire in 1615, but was rebuilt immediately by [[Shogun]] [[Tokugawa Hidetada]] and reconstructed in 1633 by [[Tokugawa Iemitsu]]. In 1651, [[Ii Naotaka]] of [[Hikone Domain]] granted it estates of 150 ''[[koku]]'' for its upkeep. However, much of the shrine was destroyed again by fire in 1733, and by a storm in 1791. Each time, it was rebuilt with the assistance of the [[Tokugawa shogunate]] and Hikone Domain. With the establishment of [[State Shinto]] following the [[Meiji restoration]], the shrine was designated a prefectural shrine under the [[Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines]] in 1871. It was promoted to a {{nihongo3|Imperial shrine, 2nd rank|官幣中社|[[Kanpei Chūsha]]}} in 1885 and to a {{nihongo3|Imperial shrine, 1st rank|官幣大社|Kanpei-taisha}} in 1914. Many of the shrine's buildings are Registered Tangible Cultural Properties of Taga Town.{{cite web |title=Registered Cultural Properties |url=https://www.town.taga.lg.jp/akebono/bunkazai/shitei.html |website=Taga Town Cultural Properties Center |publisher=Taga Town}}{{in lang|ja}} ==Festivals== *{{nihongo|'''Korei-taisai'''|古例大祭}}: Most important festival held on April 22. Also known as {{nihongo|'''Taga-matsuri'''|多賀まつり}}. *{{nihongo|'''Otaue-sai'''|御田植祭}}: A festival of planting rice with traditional rites held on June 7. *{{nihongo|'''Mantō-sai'''|万灯祭}}: A lantern festival held on the night of August 3–5. ==Specialty goods== *{{nihongo|'''Otaga-shakushi'''|お多賀杓子}}: A talisman ''shakushi'' (Japanese [[Ladle (spoon)|ladle]]) from the reign of [[Empress Genshō]]. Allegedly the origin of the Japanese word ''[[tadpole|otamajakushi]]''. *{{nihongo|'''Itokiri-mochi'''|糸切餅}}: A ''[[wagashi|Japanese sweet]]'' made of ''[[mochi]]'' sticky rice sold at souvenir shops around the shrine. ''Itokiri-mochi'' was first made to celebrate the [[Mongol invasions of Japan|Japanese victory over the Mongols]].{{Citation needed|date=November 2009}} ==Access== The shrine is about a 20 minute walk from [[Taga Taisha-mae Station]] on the [[Ohmi Railway]] [[Ohmi Railway Taga Line|Taga Line]], or about ten minutes by bus from [[Minami-Hikone Station]] on the [[West Japan Railway Company|JR West]] [[Biwako Line]]. ==See also== *[[Takamiya-juku]], the closest of the [[69 Stations of the Nakasendō]] to the shrine ==References== {{Reflist}} ==External links== {{Commons Category|Taga-taisha}} * {{Use dmy dates|date=July 2019}} {{Infobox religious building | name = Taga-taisha
多賀大社 | image = Taga taisha1.JPG | caption = ''[[Honden]]'' of Taga-taisha | map_type = Japan Shiga Prefecture#Japan | coordinates = {{Coord|35|13|27|N|136|17|26|E|region:JP_scale:30000_source:jawiki|display=title,inline}} | map_relief = 1 | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | deity = [[Izanagi]], [[Izanami]] | established = Pre-[[Nara period]] | location = 604 Taga, Inukami-gun, Shiga-ken 522-0341 | website = {{URL|http://www.tagataisya.or.jp/}} | footnotes ={{box|background=white|align=center|wide=yes|border size=3px|border color=green|text align=center|[[Monuments of Japan|National Palace of Scenic Beauty]]}} }} [[File:Taga-taisha Haiden-b.jpg|thumb|280px|Haiden]] {{nihongo|'''Taga-taisha'''|多賀大社}} is a [[Shinto]] [[Jinja (Shinto)|shrine]] located in the town of [[Taga, Shiga|Taga]], [[Inukami District, Shiga|Inukami District]], [[Shiga Prefecture]], [[Japan]]. The shrine is frequently referred to as {{nihongo|O-Taga-san|お多賀さん}} by local residents.{{cite web |title=Taga Taisha official home page |url=http://www.tagataisya.or.jp/about/ |website=About Us |publisher=Taga Taisha}}{{in lang|ja}} The gardens of the inner ''[[shoin]]'', which date to the [[Momoyama period]] are a [[Cultural Properties of Japan|nationally designated]] [[Monuments of Japan|Place of Scenic Beauty]],{{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/401/1575 |archive-url=https://archive.today/20120803171529/https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/401/1575 |url-status=dead |archive-date=3 August 2012 |title=多賀神社奥書院庭園 |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |access-date=10 February 2012 }}{{Cite web|title=THE 10 BEST Things to Do Near Taga Taisha, Taga-cho - Tripadvisor|url=https://www.tripadvisor.com/AttractionsNear-g1121270-d1992498-Taga_Taisha-Taga_cho_Inukami_gun_Shiga_Prefecture_Kinki.html|access-date=2021-06-05|website=www.tripadvisor.com|language=en}} whereas the mid-[[Edo period]] ''Shoin'' itself is a Shiga Prefectural Important Cultural Property. ==Main ''kami''== * [[Izanagi]] * [[Izanami]] [[Category:Beppyo shrines]] [[Category:qq]] [[sn:Taga-taisha]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:qq]] [[Category:qq]] [[Category:bgoodbye $1]] ==History== The foundation of Taga Taisha is unknown. It is mentioned in the ancient {{Lang|ja-latn|[[Kojiki]]}} and {{Lang|ja-latn|[[Nihon Shoki]]}} chronicles from 712 AD. The shrine's legend connects it with the legendary hero [[Yamato Takeru]], whose son, [[Inugami no Mitasuki]] was sent as an emissary to [[Japanese missions to Sui China|Sui China]] in 614 AD and the first [[Japanese missions to Tang China|Japanese embassy to Tang China]] in 630 AD. He was also the ancestor of the Inukami clan, from which [[Inukami District, Shiga]] (where the shrine is located) takes its name. However, in the 914 AD ''[[Engishiki]]'' records, it is listed only as a small shrine. From the [[Heian period]] it was revered as having efficiency for childbirth, vocational aid, fire extinguishing and longevity. During then [[Muromachi period]], as pilgrimages to the [[Ise Grand Shrine]] and the [[Kumano shrine|Kumano Sanzan]] gained in popularity, pilgrims were encouraged to visit Taga Shrine as part of the route. In the [[Sengoku period]], [[Toyotomi Hideyoshi]] had a strong faith in this shrine and frequently prayed for an extension of his mother's life. He donated 10,000 ''[[koku]]'', or the equivalent revenue of one year for a ''[[daimyō]]'' for its reconstruction and upkeep. The shrine was destroyed by a fire in 1615, but was rebuilt immediately by [[Shogun]] [[Tokugawa Hidetada]] and reconstructed in 1633 by [[Tokugawa Iemitsu]]. In 1651, [[Ii Naotaka]] of [[Hikone Domain]] granted it estates of 150 ''[[koku]]'' for its upkeep. However, much of the shrine was destroyed again by fire in 1733, and by a storm in 1791. Each time, it was rebuilt with the assistance of the [[Tokugawa shogunate]] and Hikone Domain. With the establishment of [[State Shinto]] following the [[Meiji restoration]], the shrine was designated a prefectural shrine under the [[Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines]] in 1871. It was promoted to a {{nihongo3|Imperial shrine, 2nd rank|官幣中社|[[Kanpei Chūsha]]}} in 1885 and to a {{nihongo3|Imperial shrine, 1st rank|官幣大社|Kanpei-taisha}} in 1914. Many of the shrine's buildings are Registered Tangible Cultural Properties of Taga Town.{{cite web |title=Registered Cultural Properties |url=https://www.town.taga.lg.jp/akebono/bunkazai/shitei.html |website=Taga Town Cultural Properties Center |publisher=Taga Town}}{{in lang|ja}} ==Festivals== *{{nihongo|'''Korei-taisai'''|古例大祭}}: Most important festival held on April 22. Also known as {{nihongo|'''Taga-matsuri'''|多賀まつり}}. *{{nihongo|'''Otaue-sai'''|御田植祭}}: A festival of planting rice with traditional rites held on June 7. *{{nihongo|'''Mantō-sai'''|万灯祭}}: A lantern festival held on the night of August 3–5. ==Specialty goods== *{{nihongo|'''Otaga-shakushi'''|お多賀杓子}}: A talisman ''shakushi'' (Japanese [[Ladle (spoon)|ladle]]) from the reign of [[Empress Genshō]]. Allegedly the origin of the Japanese word ''[[tadpole|otamajakushi]]''. *{{nihongo|'''Itokiri-mochi'''|糸切餅}}: A ''[[wagashi|Japanese sweet]]'' made of ''[[mochi]]'' sticky rice sold at souvenir shops around the shrine. ''Itokiri-mochi'' was first made to celebrate the [[Mongol invasions of Japan|Japanese victory over the Mongols]].{{Citation needed|date=November 2009}} ==Access== The shrine is about a 20 minute walk from [[Taga Taisha-mae Station]] on the [[Ohmi Railway]] [[Ohmi Railway Taga Line|Taga Line]], or about ten minutes by bus from [[Minami-Hikone Station]] on the [[West Japan Railway Company|JR West]] [[Biwako Line]]. ==See also== *[[Takamiya-juku]], the closest of the [[69 Stations of the Nakasendō]] to the shrine ==References== {{Reflist}} ==External links== {{Commons Category|Taga-taisha}} *
